short_description: Afar is an Afro-Asiatic language of the Cushitic branch. It is spoken in the Horn of Africa by communities associated with the Afar language group, and its linguistic classification is reflected in its categorization as Afar language in lexical reference collections.

Proto-Austroasiatic is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Austroasiatic language family. It is not directly attested; evidence comes from comparative reconstruction across its descendant languages such as Mon-Khmer and Munda. These reconstructions aim to explain shared phonological and lexical features.

Abkhaz is a Northwest Caucasian language spoken in Abkhazia, a region on the Black Sea coast of Georgia. It is used by communities in that area and is indexed among the Abkhaz language varieties in reference works, reflecting its status as a distinct language within the Caucasian linguistic landscape.

Abenaki is an Algonquian language of the Abenaki people in the northeastern portion of North America. It belongs to the Algonquian language family and has historical and continued use among Indigenous communities in present-day Canada and the United States.

Abaza is a language of the Northwest Caucasian language family spoken by the Abaza people in the Caucasus region. It is documented in linguistic reference works and classified under the Abaza language category; available materials describe its existence and classification but do not provide precise contemporary speaker counts or a fixed historical period.

Ambonese Malay is a Malay language of the Ambon Islands in Indonesia. It is part of the Malayic branch of the Austronesian language family and is used in the Ambon region; it functions as a regional language for everyday communication among local communities.

Baharna Arabic is a variety of Arabic within the Afro-Asiatic language family. It represents a regional form of Arabic with distinctive phonological and lexical features compared to other dialects, and it is documented as the Baharna Arabic language in linguistic references.

Inabaknon is an Austronesian language spoken on Capul Island in Northern Samar, Philippines. Its listing places it within the wider Austronesian family, without asserting a precise sub-branch, while providing a concise framework for etymology-focused reference.

Acehnese is an Austronesian language in the Malayic branch spoken in Aceh, a province on the northern tip of Sumatra, Indonesia. It is a living language with its own dialects and historical attestations, part of the wider Malayic languages within the Austronesian family.

Acholi is a language identified by the entry named Acholi language in a linguistic category. It is part of the African language landscape documented in this resource, with entries for related terms and linguistic notes grouped under the Acholi language category.

Iraqi Arabic is a language entry associated with the Iraqi Arabic language category. It reflects an Arabic variety documented within the broader Iraqi Arabic language group, focusing on how related forms are organized under this label.

Hijazi Arabic is a variety of the Arabic language spoken in the Hejaz region of western Saudi Arabia. It is part of the Afro-Asiatic language family and the Semitic branch; as a regional Arabic variety, it shows distinctive phonology and vocabulary while sharing core Arabic grammar.

Omani Arabic is a variety of the Arabic language. It is linked to the region of Oman and listed within the Omani Arabic language category, reflecting regional linguistic variation within the broader Arabic language family.

Cypriot Arabic is a variety of Arabic within the Afro-Asiatic language family spoken in Cyprus. It represents a regional form of Arabic associated with Cypriot communities, illustrating Cyprus’s place within the broader Arabic linguistic lineage.

Adi is a Sino-Tibetan language spoken by the Adi people in Arunachal Pradesh, northeast India. It is classified within the Tani subgroup, and its history is understood through comparison with nearby languages rather than direct attestation from ancient records.

West Circassian is a Circassian language of the Northwest Caucasian family. It is associated with the Caucasus region and reflects linguistic relationships rather than contemporary usage. Its place within the Circassian subgroup suggests close kinship to other Circassian varieties and to historical migrations in the region.

Avestan is an extinct Eastern Iranian language, a branch of the Indo-Iranian sub-division of the Indo-European family. It is the language of the Avesta, the Zoroastrian scriptures, historically associated with the ancient Iranian plateau and scriptural transmission.

Tunisian Arabic is a variety of Arabic spoken in Tunisia by local communities. It belongs to the Afro-Asiatic language family and is used primarily in everyday speech within the Tunisian region. It shows regional variation in pronunciation and vocabulary compared with other Arabic varieties.

Northeast Pashayi is a language in the Pashayi language group of Afghanistan. It belongs to the Indo-Iranian branch of the Indo-European family and is associated with the Northeast Pashayi sub-branch in linguistic classifications. It is used by communities in the region and has historical documentation in linguistic literature.

Eastern Arrernte is an Aboriginal language of central Australia. It belongs to the Arrernte language group within Australian languages and has historical use in the central Australian region, with features typical of Indigenous Australian language systems.

Afrikaans is a West Germanic language in the Indo-European family, descended from Dutch and closely related to Dutch. It is spoken in southern Africa, especially South Africa and Namibia, and developed during the 17th century as Dutch colonial contact and creolization shaped its form.

Proto-Afroasiatic is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Afro-Asiatic language family. It is not directly attested. The status reflects scholarly inference rather than a written record, and it serves as a working hypothesis for understanding the historical relationships among Afro-Asiatic languages.

Gulf Arabic is a variety of Arabic in the Afro-Asiatic language family, spoken in the Gulf region of the Arabian Peninsula and nearby areas. It is described as a regional spoken variety with distinct phonological and lexical features used in daily communication.

Argobba is a human language listed under the Argobba language category. It is part of the Afro-Asiatic language family and is associated with the Horn of Africa region, with historical documentation indicating its use in past and possibly present communities.

Ahom is a historical Tai language spoken by the Ahom people in the Assam region of northeast India. It is identified as part of the Tai language family and is primarily known from historical documentation rather than contemporary daily use.

Ahirani is a language recognized under the label Ahirani language. It is treated as a distinct language in linguistic references, but the available source does not specify its status, regional range, or historical period beyond being listed as a language category.

Ahtna is an Athabaskan language of Alaska. It forms part of the Athabaskan language family and is associated with the Ahtna people in the Alaska region. Documentation notes its classification within the language family and its regional identity, without asserting contemporary usage or exact speaker numbers.

Antigua and Barbuda Creole English is an English-based creole language spoken in Antigua and Barbuda in the Caribbean. It forms part of the English-based Creole family and is used in everyday speech alongside Standard English in that country.

Ai-Cham is a language of the Ai-Cham people in Southeast Asia. The available category listing identifies Ai-Cham as a language, with no further details on its linguistic family, regional distribution beyond Southeast Asia, or historical period.

Assyrian Neo-Aramaic is a living Aramaic language in the Afro-Asiatic family. It is spoken by Assyrian communities in parts of the Middle East, including northern Iraq, southeastern Turkey, northeastern Syria, and western Iran, with modern usage dating to contemporary periods.

Ainu is an indigenous language of the Ainu people in Japan, historically centered in Hokkaido. It is classified as the Ainu language in linguistic references and has limited modern use. Efforts focus on documentation and revival among younger speakers.

Aiton is a language identified by its name in linguistic descriptions. It is treated as a distinct language with classifications linking it to a historical language family and a geographic region, described within a comparative framework and noting its approximate historical period.

South Levantine Arabic is a language variety within the Arabic branch of the Afro-Asiatic family. It is associated with the southern Levant region in the Middle East and forms part of the Levantine Arabic group. The designation treats it as a named language variety rather than a separate language with a distinct, documented historical trajectory.

Akan is a language listed in the Akan language category. It forms part of a West African linguistic area and is associated with the Akan people. The entry provides basic lexical and grammatical information for etymology research.

Akawaio is a language categorized under Akawaio language. It is documented with lexical data and entries that help distinguish its vocabulary and linguistic features. The entry serves as a reference point for researchers examining related languages within the same language group and for describing its lexical items in comparative studies.

An ancient Afro-Asiatic language of Mesopotamia, Akkadian is attested from the early 3rd millennium BCE to the end of antiquity. It belongs to the Semitic branch of Afro-Asiatic and exists in multiple regional varieties documented in cuneiform texts.

Aklanon is a language of the Philippines. It is recognized as a distinct language within the Austronesian language family, spoken by communities in the region around Aklan. Its status is that of a living language used in education and daily communication by speakers within local communities.

Akhvakh is a Northeast Caucasian language of the Andic group, spoken in Dagestan, Russia. It belongs to the broader Caucasian language family and is used by local communities in the region, with its history rooted in the area rather than extending into a long attested classical tradition.

Alabama is a Muskogean language of the Southeastern United States, historically spoken by the Alabama people. It is part of the Muskogean language family; associated with the Alabama community in the present-day southeastern U.S. region, with historical use documented in traditional territories.

Aleut is a language represented in linguistic reference materials. Its entry is categorized under Aleut language in a lexical database, guiding researchers in tracing word origins and historical development within this language group. This description remains general and non-specific.

Proto-Algonquian is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Algonquian language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from the comparative study of its descendant languages. As a proto-language, it represents historical connections among Algonquian languages, rather than an individual, documented speech community.

Alangan is an Austronesian language of the Philippines. It belongs to the Malayo-Polynesian and Philippine branches, reflecting its place within the broader Austronesian language family as part of the country’s indigenous linguistic landscape.

Southern Altai is a Turkic language of the Turkic language family, spoken in the Altai region of Russia. It is recognized as a distinct language within the Southern Altai group, with historical usage and documentation in linguistic sources related to the Altai peoples.

Proto-Edekiri is the reconstructed ancestor of the Edekiri language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred by comparative method from descendant Edekiri languages. This proto-language represents the shared phonological and lexical features hypothesized for the prehistory of Edekiri varieties.

Proto-Edoid is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Edoid language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative evidence among its descendant Edoid languages, reconstructing phonology, lexicon, and grammar to explain shared features across the group.

Proto-Central Togo is a reconstructed proto-language not directly attested, identified as the common ancestor of languages in the Central Togo region. It is studied through comparative evidence and historical linguistics, with the reconstruction reflecting shared phonological and lexical features of descendant Togo languages.

Proto-Igboid is the reconstructed ancestor of Igboid languages. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative analysis of descendant Igboid languages. The reconstruction documents shared features and regular correspondences used to explain the development of Igboid languages from a common source.

Proto-Kwa is the reconstructed ancestor of the Kwa language family, a group of languages spoken in West Africa. It is not directly attested, having been inferred from shared features of later Kwa branches, with an estimated time frame situated before documented Kwa diversification.

Proto-Nupoid is the reconstructed ancestor of the Nupoid language family. It is not directly attested; reconstructions are made by comparing cognate features across descendant Nupoid languages and tracing systematic sound correspondences. This entry reflects the historical stage reconstructed for the family, rather than a documented language with preserved texts.

Proto-Atlantic-Congo is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Atlantic-Congo language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred through comparative linguistic methods, describing a proto-language that gave rise to a wide range of descendant Atlantic-Congo languages.

Proto-Volta-Niger is a reconstructed proto-language that serves as the common ancestor of the Volta-Niger languages. It is not directly attested, and its features are inferred from comparisons of descendant languages within the Niger-Congo family. Scholars reconstruct sound correspondences and shared vocabulary to outline its structure.

Proto-Yoruba is a reconstructed language not directly attested. It is the ancestral stage of the Yoruba languages, identified through comparative reconstruction of their descendants and linguistic features. The form is inferred, and its exact period is approximate rather than precisely dated.

Proto-Yoruboid is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Yoruboid languages. It is not directly attested and is inferred through comparative reconstruction from descendant languages such as Yoruba and related tongues. Scholars use systematic sound correspondences and lexical comparisons to outline its likely phonology and lexicon.

Alaba is a language identified by an entry in the Alaba language category. The available information does not specify its status, linguistic family, region, or historical period, so the description remains noncommittal and avoids unsupported claims.

Amharic is a living Afro-Asiatic language of the Semitic branch. It belongs to the Ethiopian Semitic subgroup within Afro-Asiatic and is spoken across diverse regions of Ethiopia, with multiple dialects reflecting its long, historical development.

Amis is an Austronesian language spoken by the Amis people of Taiwan. It is part of Taiwan's indigenous linguistic heritage and is documented in linguistic references as a distinct language within the Austronesian family today.

Guerrero Amuzgo is a language of the Amuzgo branch of the Oto-Manguean family, spoken in the Guerrero region of Mexico. It is recorded in linguistic references as part of the Guerrero Amuzgo language group and is not described with precise speaker counts.

Aragonese is a language in the Romance language family. It is historically associated with the Aragon region in northern Spain and has medieval roots in the Iberian linguistic landscape, reflected in its traditional vocabulary, phonology, and orthography.

Xârâcùù is a language named Xârâcùù, classified within the Northeast Caucasian language family and spoken in the Caucasus region. The available material groups it with related Caucasian languages, but it does not provide detailed information on its current status, precise regional coverage, or historical period.

Old English is a West Germanic language historically spoken in England from the early medieval period. It is the early form of the English language, ancestral to Middle and Modern English, and is primarily attested in Old English texts from roughly the 5th to the 11th century in England and in parts of Britain.

Anglian Old English is a historical form of Old English, and a member of the Germanic language family. It is linked to the Anglian subgroup and the early medieval Anglia region, contributing distinctive vocabulary to the language family during that period.

Northumbrian Old English is a historical stage of the Old English language tied to the Northumbria region in northern England during the early medieval period. It is attested through linguistic terms derived from that variety and serves as a reference point for studying Old English dialectal variation.

Andi is a human language recognized in Wiktionary’s Andi language category. It has entries that document its name and related lexical items for etymology research, helping establish linguistic connections and word histories within its language group.

Angolar is a language spoken on the island of São Tomé in São Tomé and Príncipe. It is a Portuguese-based creole that developed in contact between Portuguese and local languages, reflecting the Atlantic island linguistic landscape.

Pemon is a language categorized under the Pemon language group. It is treated in linguistic resources as a distinct language with etymological entries that explore word origins, loanwords, and historical forms within its language family and regional usage.

Atong (India) is a language of India documented in linguistic resources. It is associated with the Atong language category for India, reflecting its recognition within regional language classifications. The entry notes its status as a human language and situates it within India's diverse linguistic landscape.

North Levantine Arabic is a dialect of Arabic spoken in the North Levant region, including parts of Syria and Lebanon. It belongs to the Afro-Asiatic language family, within the Semitic branch, and is used in everyday communication among regional communities in contemporary times.

Sudanese Arabic is a dialect of Arabic spoken in Sudan. It belongs to the Afro-Asiatic language family, within the Semitic branch, and is used across Sudan in urban and rural communities, displaying regional variation within the dialect continuum.

Jicarilla is a language of the Jicarilla Apache people. It belongs to the Uto-Aztecan language family and is associated with the Jicarilla region in present-day northern New Mexico. The entry emphasizes its linguistic classification rather than detailed notes on modern usage.

Western Apache is an Athabaskan language spoken by the Western Apache peoples in the southwestern United States, especially Arizona. It forms part of the broader Athabaskan language family and is represented in linguistic documentation that catalogs its lexical and grammatical features.

Apalaí is a language of the Amazon region in Brazil. The entry places it within the Apalaí language category, reflecting its identification as a distinct human language; there is no claim of revival or specific dialects here, and it is presented as a discrete linguistic entity for reference.

Proto-Algic is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Algic language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparisons of descendant languages within Algic, including Algonquian and related branches. Reconstruction aims to explain shared phonology, morphology, and lexicon across the family.

Arabic is an Afro-Asiatic language in the Semitic branch, spoken across the Arab world in the Middle East and North Africa. It encompasses a range of varieties used for administration, scholarship, and daily communication, reflecting a long-standing regional linguistic presence and diverse regional communities.

Aramaic is a Semitic language of the Afro-Asiatic family with historical attestations in the Near East. It has produced a variety of dialects and textual traditions spanning many centuries. Scholars study its forms across ancient inscriptions and later manuscripts.

Imperial Aramaic is an ancient Aramaic language in the Afro-Asiatic family, used in the Near East. It is a historical stage that gave rise to subsequent Aramaic varieties and contributed vocabulary to many languages in the region.

Arhuaco is a documented language of the Arhuaco people in northern Colombia. It is part of the Chibchan language family and has linguistic entries that classify it within an Indigenous regional context. The available material focuses on taxonomy and regional association rather than contemporary speaker data.

Arikara refers to a human language identified by a language entry under the Arikara language category. The available information lists the name Arikara and associates it with language documentation, but provides no explicit details about its status, linguistic family, geographic region, or historical period.

Mapudungun is an Indigenous language of the Mapuche people in southern Chile and western Argentina. It is part of the Araucanian language family and is documented in various historical sources from the colonial era onward.

Arapaho is a human language spoken by the Arapaho people in North America, with a documented history and linguistic study. Etymology entries describe word origins and historical forms associated with this language, reflecting its documented vocabulary and usage.

Algerian Arabic is a language variety categorized as Algerian Arabic language and spoken in Algeria. It is a form of Arabic used in daily communication among Algerian communities, reflecting regional variation across the country. The listing emphasizes its status as a distinct language variety rather than a mere dialect.

Lokono is an Arawakan language of the Lokonoan branch. It is spoken in northern South America, with attested varieties historically used by communities in Guyana and nearby areas. The language is documented in lexicographic and linguistic resources that classify it within the Arawakan family.

Moroccan Arabic is a variety of Arabic in the Afro-Asiatic language family, spoken in Morocco. It forms part of the Maghrebi Arabic group and is used as a primary regional spoken language in everyday life.

Egyptian Arabic is a variety of the Afro-Asiatic language family spoken in Egypt. It belongs to the Arabic branch and is the regional form of the language used in daily life, with distinct pronunciation, vocabulary, and syntax compared to Modern Standard Arabic.

Assamese is an Indo-Aryan language of the Eastern Indic branch spoken primarily in Assam, in the northeast of India. It has a long literary tradition in the region and is used across education, media, and daily life.

American Sign Language is a natural sign language used by Deaf communities in the United States and parts of Canada. As a member of sign languages, it has a separate linguistic system that exists alongside spoken languages in the region.

Asilulu is a human language categorized as the Asilulu language. The provided source does not specify its status, family, region, or historical period; no direct attestation or reconstruction is described in this entry. Further details would require consulting the original category.

Asturian is an Ibero-Romance language of the Western Romance subgroup within the Indo-European family. It is spoken in the region of Asturias in northwestern Spain and is part of the continuum of Asturleonese language varieties.

As is a language entry listed under the Category:As_language. The available material provides no explicit information on its linguistic family, region, or time period, so the entry remains a minimal label without attested classification or historical context in this source set.

Zaiwa is a Sino-Tibetan language spoken in parts of Myanmar and China. It is considered a distinct language within its regional linguistic grouping, with limited historical documentation and no widely attested status beyond traditional usage in local communities.

Proto-Athabaskan is the reconstructed ancestor of the Athabaskan language family. It is not directly attested and is established by comparative linguistic methods, serving as the basis for understanding shared phonology, grammar, and lexicon among descendant Athabaskan languages.

Proto-Pama-Nyungan is the reconstructed ancestor of the Pama-Nyungan language family and is not directly attested. Its features are inferred by systematic comparison of the descendant languages within the family. This reconstruction supports understanding of historical relationships and linguistic change among related varieties.

Avar is a language with an entry in the Avar language category. The available material concentrates on its linguistic terminology and classification within language studies, without asserting details about speakers, region, or period at present.

Awadhi is a language identified by a linguistic category entry. It is a human language used to encode vocabulary, grammar, and meaning for communication within communities that speak it. The entry anchors its existence in a formal linguistic catalog without providing further ethnographic or regional detail.

Proto-Arawak is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Arawakan languages. It is not directly attested; its existence is inferred from comparative evidence across descendant languages in the Caribbean and northern South America, forming the basis for the Arawakan language family.

Araweté is an indigenous language of Brazil. It is associated with the Araweté language category in lexical references, which collect its words and morphologies for study. The entry reflects its status as a human language with historical use among indigenous communities in the region, though detailed sociolinguistic information is not provided here.

Middle Armenian is a historical stage of the Armenian language within the Indo-European family. It is used in linguistic classifications to distinguish earlier forms of Armenian from later developments, representing a distinct historical phase rather than a contemporary variety.
Aymara is a language in the Aymaran language family. It is spoken in the Andes region of South America, primarily in Bolivia, Peru, and Chile.

Libyan Arabic is a variety of the Arabic language spoken in Libya. It forms part of the Afro-Asiatic language family and falls within the Northwest Arabic group associated with Maghrebi speech in North Africa. It is used in daily discourse and media within Libyan communities.

Yemeni Arabic is a variety of the Afro-Asiatic language family within the Semitic branch, spoken in Yemen. It forms a regional Arabic dialect variety with distinctive phonology and vocabulary used in Yemen and nearby areas.

Azerbaijani is the language listed under Wiktionary’s Azerbaijani language category. The entry serves as a repository for etymology and word formation related to Azerbaijani, linking related terms and their historical roots. The category grouping signals a recognized language associated with the name, with lexical items organized through standard etymological entries.

Proto-Nahuan is a reconstructed language, the common ancestor of the Nahuan branch of the Uto-Aztecan language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparison of descendant languages such as Nahuatl, with features reconstructed rather than observed.

Proto-Uto-Aztecan is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Uto-Aztecan language family. It is not directly attested; knowledge about it comes from comparative reconstruction of descendant languages, including Nahuan and Uto-Aztecan branches, rather than from direct written records.

Proto-Takic is a reconstructed proto-language and not directly attested. It represents the reconstructed ancestor of the Takic languages, established through comparative reconstruction among descendant varieties and used to explain shared phonological and lexical features. It is treated as a scholarly construct rather than a directly documented speech.

San Pedro Amuzgos Amuzgo is an Amuzgo language spoken in the Oaxaca region of Mexico; it belongs to the Amuzgo language family and is attested in linguistic resources describing this language. The description emphasizes its historical usage and regional variation.

Highland Puebla Nahuatl is a Nahuatl language variety spoken in the highlands of Puebla, Mexico. It is part of the Nahuatl language family and represents one of the Puebla-area varieties documented as a distinct language in linguistic references.

Bashkir is a Turkic language spoken by the Bashkir people in the Bashkortostan region of Russia. It belongs to the Turkic language family and uses its own standard varieties for everyday communication and literary expression within Bashkir communities.

Bahamian Creole is an English-based creole language spoken in The Bahamas. It forms part of the English-based creole family of Caribbean languages and arose through contact between English and African language varieties during the colonial era in the Caribbean.

Baluchi is an Iranian language listed under the Baluchi language category. It belongs to the Indo-Iranian language family and is traditionally associated with the Baluchistan region across parts of Iran, Pakistan, and nearby areas.

Balinese is a living Austronesian language of the Malayo-Polynesian branch, spoken on the island of Bali in Indonesia. It is used in everyday speech and traditional cultural contexts, including temple rites and literature, and has historical development within the Bali-Sunda region.

Bavarian is a West Germanic dialect of the German language, belonging to the Indo-European family. It is spoken in Bavaria in southern Germany and in parts of Austria, with varieties that form a continuous dialect continuum rather than a sharply defined standard.

Proto-Baltic is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Baltic language group within the Indo-European family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from later Baltic languages such as Lithuanian and Latvian; its reconstruction places it in the Baltic region of northern Europe in the prehistoric period.

Baatonum is a language categorized under Baatonum language on Wiktionary. It is associated with West Africa and is used in linguistic discussions as a named language, with the classification reflecting its historical and cultural name usage.

Toba Batak is a language of the Batak branch within the Austronesian language family. It is spoken in North Sumatra, Indonesia, by communities identifying as Toba Batak and is considered a living language with active use among speakers.

Bats is a language listed in the Bats_language category. The available material identifies it as a language entry within this category, but provides no further information on its status, linguistic family, region, or historical period. Consequently, its classification details remain unspecified in the cited category entry.

Central Bikol is a language identified by the name Central Bikol. It is listed in linguistic resources under the Central Bikol language category, indicating it as a distinct language variety recognized in reference works globally.

Budukh is a language listed in the Budukh language category on Wiktionary. It is described as part of the Caucasian language area, with its classification within a broader Northeast Caucasian family and historical use inferred from linguistic records.
Indonesian Bajau is a language in the Bajau language group. It is listed in a Wiktionary category dedicated to the Indonesian Bajau language.

West Coast Bajau is a language categorized as West Coast Bajau language. It is a Bajau variant listed in a linguistic category, indicating a distinct language variety associated with coastal regions. The source does not provide speaker numbers or a precise historical period.

Bandjalang is a language listed in the Bandjalang language category, indicating its recognition as a named language in linguistic references. It appears among related languages and terminology used to document its classification and historical connections within the wider Australian language landscape.

Belarusian is an East Slavic language spoken in Belarus and surrounding areas. It belongs to the Slavic branch of the Indo-European family and has a long literary and cultural history tied to the Belarusian region.

Beja is a human language associated with the Beja people. It is categorized as Beja language in a lexical reference, indicating recognition as a distinct language within its regional context. The entry reflects its status as a documented language rather than a dialect, without asserting broader sociolinguistic conditions.

Proto-Berber is the reconstructed ancestor of the Berber languages in the Afro-Asiatic family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from later Berber languages spoken in North Africa. Its status is scholarly reconstruction rather than documentary evidence.

Betawi is a Malay-based language of the Austronesian language family spoken in Jakarta and the surrounding region in Indonesia. It is part of the Malayic subgroup and is described as a separate language within the broader Malay-Indonesia linguistic landscape.

Mmen is a language categorized as Mmen language. The available entry provides only its name and a category placement, with no additional details about linguistic family, region, status, or historical period.

Bulgarian is a Slavic language in the Indo-European family. It is spoken in Bulgaria and by diaspora communities, with a history rooted in the South Slavic branch and a standard literary form established in the modern era.

Bihari refers to the Bihari language category. It encompasses related Eastern Indo-Aryan languages within the Indo-European family. Varieties are grouped under the Bihari language category, serving as a labeling convention for a set of related languages rather than a single uniform language.

Bhili is an Indo-Aryan language belonging to the Bhil subgroup of Western Indo-Aryan. It is spoken by Bhil communities in western and central India, with its historical development continuing through traditional dialects and regional varieties.

Bukhari is listed as a language under the Bukhari language category. The entry is used in etymology resources to group related terms and linguistic data, without asserting a specific status, geographic region, or historical period. The available record provides no direct attestation or identity details beyond its name.

Bhojpuri is an Indo-Arian language of the Indo-European family spoken in the Bhojpuri region of northern India and parts of Nepal. It has regional varieties and is widely used in local media and daily communication.

Biak is a language categorized under the Biak language category. It is recognized as a distinct language within its language group. Regions and status are not detailed here, and no specific historical period is provided beyond its categorization.

Bislama is an English-based creole language spoken in Vanuatu. It developed as a contact language in the Pacific and is part of the English-based creole family. It is used for everyday communication across communities in Vanuatu and serves as a working lingua franca alongside other local languages.

Edo is a language in the Edoid subgroup of the Niger-Congo language family, spoken in Nigeria. It is regarded as a distinct language within the Edoid linguistic region, with documented lexical items and grammatical features that illustrate its structure and historical development.

Kannauji is a language listed under the Kannauji language category in linguistic reference materials. It represents a named language with entries organized by name rather than by specific scripts, regions, or speakers. The description remains neutral and avoids unsupported details.

Banjarese is the language of the Banjar people, primarily spoken in Indonesia. It is part of the Austronesian language family and is associated with the Kalimantan region. The language is used for everyday communication within Banjar communities and has historical and cultural significance in the area.

Bedjond is a language recognized in linguistic references as Bedjond language. It is cataloged as a distinct language label for organizing vocabulary and etymology data, without detailed information about its language family, region, or historical period in the cited material.

Binukid is a language listed in the Binukid language category. The reference material groups it with other entries under this label, indicating its status as a named language in linguistic documentation. No additional details about origin, classification, or usage are provided here.

Masbate Sorsogon is an Austronesian language spoken in the Philippines, in the Masbate and Sorsogon regions. It belongs to the Philippine branch within the broader Austronesian language family, with its forms catalogued in language-resource categories for this region.

Pa’o Karen is a Karen language in the Sino-Tibetan family. It belongs to the Karenic branch of Tibeto-Burman and is associated with Karen-speaking communities in Southeast Asia. The classification and terminology reflect its relation to related Pa’o and Karen languages.

Southern Catanduanes Bikol is a Bikol language of the Philippines, belonging to the Southern Catanduanes subgroup within the Bikol branch. It is associated with the southern Catanduanes region in the Bicol peninsula, reflecting its regional usage within that area.

Tai Dam is a language in the Tai–Kadai language family. It is categorized specifically as Tai Dam in linguistic classifications, illustrating its place in the broader Tai language group. This entry emphasizes classification and lineage rather than concrete speakers or locations.

Bambara is a language in the Mande group of the Niger-Congo language family, spoken in Mali. It is a distinct language with its own vocabulary and grammar within the Mande languages, reflecting typical features of the regional language varieties.

Bengali is a language identified by the name Bengali and listed in a category dedicated to the Bengali language. The entry serves as a hub for etymological data and related terms connected to its lexicon and word origins.

Bunun is a Formosan language of the Austronesian family spoken in Taiwan by the Bunun people; it is part of the Formosan subgroup and attested in historical and contemporary records. Its exact classification varies in scholarly sources, but it is widely considered related to other Formosan languages.

Asi is a language categorized under the Asi language group. It is documented in linguistic classifications that group it with related languages in the Asi family, reflecting scholarly organization of languages rather than implying specific regions or contemporary use.

Proto-Nguni is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Nguni languages in the Bantu family of the Niger-Congo stock. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparisons of descendant languages; its exact period is not known.

Phuthi is a Bantu language of Southern Africa. It is spoken by the Phuthi people in areas around the region and is part of the broader Southern Bantu language family. Its exact status varies by country, and sources on historical periods are limited.

Proto-Bantu is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Bantu languages. It is not directly attested; its features are inferred by comparative reconstruction across the Bantu descendants. Scholars use this reconstruction to explain shared phonology, morphology, and lexicon among languages such as Swahili, Zulu, and Kongo, situating Proto-Bantu as a key node in their historical development.

Proto-Sabaki is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Sabaki language group of East Africa. It is not directly attested and is inferred through comparative reconstruction of descendant languages such as Swahili and related coastal Bantu languages.

Tibetan is a language historically documented in the Tibetan region and surrounding areas, with varieties that have existed for many centuries on the Tibetan Plateau. It is spoken in parts of China, India, and Nepal, and refers to a broad family of related dialects and literary forms.

Bole is a language name used for a human language in linguistic reference materials. This entry provides only the label without explicit information on its language family, region, or period, so its precise classification and history would require checking specialized sources for confirmation.

Bishnupriya Manipuri is a language listed in a linguistic reference under the Bishnupriya Manipuri language category. The entry groups lexical items and related terms that pertain to this language, reflecting its recognition as a distinct language within the reference work.

It is the hypothetical common ancestor of the Avaro-Andian language family, inferred through comparative methods to account for shared vocabulary and sound correspondences among descendant languages. Proto-Circassian is a reconstructed proto-language and not directly attested. It is the common ancestor of the Circassian languages, part of the Northwest Caucasian language family. Proto-Northeast Caucasian is a reconstructed proto-language and the inferred common ancestor of the Northeast Caucasian language family. It is not directly attested; its features are reconstructed by comparing descendant languages within the group.

Proto-Nakh is the reconstructed ancestor of the Nakh languages of the Northeast Caucasian family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from shared features of descendants such as Chechen and Ingush spoken in the Caucasus.

Proto-Northwest Caucasian is the reconstructed ancestor of the Northwest Caucasian language family. It is not directly attested and is understood through comparative reconstruction of descendant languages such as Abkhaz and Circassian, among others in the Northwest Caucasian group.

Proto-Tsezian is the reconstructed ancestor of the Tsezian languages in the Northeast Caucasian language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative evidence among descendants spoken in the North Caucasus region of Dagestan and surrounding areas.

Proto-Georgian-Zan is a reconstructed proto-language of the Kartvelian language family, ancestral to Georgian and Zan. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative methods. This entry labels its status clearly and avoids asserting speakers or locations.

Proto-Kartvelian is the reconstructed ancestor of the Kartvelian language family and is not directly attested. It is identified through comparative reconstruction of its descendant languages and their shared features, rather than any contemporary or direct written records.

Proto-Celtic is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Celtic languages within the Indo-European family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from later Celtic languages; its reconstruction reflects shared features across descendant languages rather than direct written records.

Kagayanen is a human language categorized in linguistic resources as the Kagayanen language. It is a distinct language with its own lexical entries illustrating vocabulary, affixes, and morphological patterns used by speakers in its regional speech community.

Chamorro is a language of the Chamorro people, represented in linguistic resources as the Chamorro language. It is part of the Austronesian language family and is associated with the Pacific region where Chamorro linguistic varieties are studied and documented.

Chibcha is a language identified within the Chibcha language category. It is a member of the Chibchan language family and is referenced in linguistic traditions as the name for this particular language. The description reflects its inclusion in a broader language classification.
