Dalmatian is a Romance language of the Indo-European family historically associated with the Dalmatia region along the Adriatic coast. It is categorized as the Dalmatian language, reflecting its traditional use in that area and its inclusion in linguistic classifications of the Romance branch.

Domaaki is a language categorized under Domaaki language in a category. The language has its own lexical items and etymological data, which are compiled in the category to support comparative etymology and historical linguistics studies.

Dungan is a Sinitic language in the Mandarin branch of the Sino-Tibetan family, spoken by the Dungan people in Central Asia. It forms part of the Mandarin group and is associated with communities in several Central Asian countries.

Proto-Central Dravidian is a reconstructed proto-language, not directly attested; it is the hypothesized ancestral form of the Central Dravidian languages within the Dravidian family, inferred from comparative evidence among its descendants. But exact features remain hypothetical.

Middle Kannada is a historical stage of the Kannada language in the Dravidian language family. It was used in southern India and is characterized by earlier literary forms and regional variation that precede the modern standard language.

Old Kannada is a historical form of the Kannada language, belonging to the Dravidian language family. It is attested in older texts and inscriptions as an early stage in the development of Kannada. The entry captures its scholarly classification and does not imply contemporary usage.

Old Telugu is a historical stage of the Telugu language, classified within the Dravidian language family. It represents an earlier form of Telugu used in historical contexts, contributing to the development of the language's phonology, grammar, and lexicon as documented in linguistic studies of the Dravidian languages in the region.

Proto-Dravidian is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Dravidian language family. It is inferred by comparing descendant languages such as Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, and Malayalam, rather than from direct written records, and it is not directly attested.

Proto-South Dravidian I is a reconstructed proto-language, not directly attested, positioned as the common ancestor of the South Dravidian languages. It is part of the Dravidian language family and is inferred to have existed in the distant past, prior to diversification of its descendant languages.

Proto-South Dravidian II is a reconstructed proto-language within the Dravidian language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative evidence among descendant South Dravidian languages, serving as an ancestral stage for later Dravidian tongues.

Proto-South Dravidian is a reconstructed proto-language and the hypothetical common ancestor of the South Dravidian language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred through comparative analysis of descendant languages such as Tamil, Telugu, Kannada, and Malayalam, with no precise period or locale.

Rungus is an Austronesian language spoken by the Rungus people in Sabah, Malaysia. It is classified within the Malayo-Polynesian subgroup of Austronesian and is used in northern Borneo communities. This description reflects the language's regional presence and ancestry.

Lela is a language listed in the Wiktionary category Lela language. The entry compiles lexical items and etymologies associated with Lela, offering researchers a cataloged reference for word origins within this language family. No speaker data or regional notes are provided.

Rukai is an Austronesian language of the Formosan branch spoken by the Rukai people in Taiwan; it forms part of the Rukai subgroup within Formosan and is documented as a distinct language within the broader Formosan language family.

Proto-Rukai is the reconstructed ancestor of the Rukai language family. It is not directly attested and is established through comparative methods among descendant Rukai languages; as a proto-language, its features are inferred rather than documented in direct records.

Lower Sorbian is a West Slavic language in the Sorbian branch of the Indo-European language family. It is part of the Sorbian languages within the West Slavic group of the Indo-European family, and its place here reflects classification rather than current usage.

Daur is a language listed under the Daur language category. This entry provides lexical data and etymological notes associated with the name, but details on its status, linguistic family, regional location, or period are not specified here.

Central Dusun is a language entry categorized as Central Dusun language. It belongs to the Dusun subgroup of the Austronesian language family, and is described in lexical resources that cover its forms and vocabulary. These resources provide etymological notes and historical connections.

Middle Dutch is a medieval stage of the Dutch language within the West Germanic branch, used in the Low Countries from roughly the 12th through the 15th century. It developed from Old Dutch and preceded Early Modern Dutch, with regional varieties attested in literature and religious texts of the period.
Dhivehi, also called Maldivian, is an Indo-Aryan language spoken in the Maldives. It is part of the Indo-Iranian branch of the Indo-European language family.

Old Dhivehi is the historical source language from which terms have been derived into other languages. It serves as the basis for etymology entries that trace words back to Old Dhivehi origins, illustrating cross-linguistic influence. The category-style records document these derivations, highlighting lexical connections without asserting contemporary usage or speakers.

Dzongkha is a living language categorized under the Dzongkha language category. It belongs to the Sino-Tibetan language family and is used in Bhutan, serving as a key language in government and education. The form reflects Tibeto-Burman features and historical development within the Himalayan linguistic area.

Ewe is a living Niger-Congo language in the Gbe subgroup of West Africa. It is spoken by communities in southeastern Togo, southern Ghana, and neighboring Benin, where it serves as a primary means of communication for daily life, culture, and local discourse.

Emilian is a Romance language spoken in the Emilia region of northern Italy. It is part of the Romance language family and has regional varieties used in traditional speech and local literature, with historical roots in medieval Italy.
Demotic Egyptian is the Demotic stage of the ancient Egyptian language. It belongs to the Afro-Asiatic language family and was used in Egypt from around the 7th century BCE to the 5th century CE.

Egyptian is an Afro-Asiatic language once spoken in ancient Egypt, with written records dating from the early dynastic period until the final centuries of antiquity; it evolved through stages such as Old Egyptian, Middle Egyptian, Late Egyptian, Demotic, and Coptic, and is now extinct.

Greek is a language categorized under the Greek language category. It is the subject of indexed entries documenting word histories and etymology within that Wiktionary category, reflecting its use across historical and regional varieties.

Katharevousa is a form of the Greek language developed in the 19th century as a purist revival of classical Greek. It functioned as an official and literary standard in Greece, primarily in the Hellenic region, alongside the vernacular demotic Greek.

Eastern Maninkakan is a Mande language of the Niger-Congo language family spoken in West Africa. It belongs to the Eastern Maninkakan subgroup within the Maninka branch and represents a regional variety of the broader Maninka languages.

Alutiiq is a language of Alaska spoken by the Sugpiaq people in the Kodiak region. It is catalogued on Wiktionary under the Alutiiq language category, reflecting its status as a distinct human language with its own vocabulary and structure.

English is a West Germanic language of the Indo-European family that originated in England and has spread worldwide as a global lingua franca. It developed through successive stages from Old English in the early medieval period to Middle and Modern English, with roots in the Germanic branch and extensive influence from Norse and Romance languages.

Australian English is a language variety of English spoken in Australia. The category collects terms derived from Australian English by language, illustrating how its vocabulary has been adapted into other languages. This entry provides a high-level etymology overview suitable for users tracing loanword paths and language contact in Australasian linguistic influence.

Early Modern English is a historical stage of the English language. It is the source of numerous terms that spread into other languages and reflects the transition between Middle English and Modern English in historical studies.

British English is a living variety of the English language spoken in the United Kingdom. It is part of the Indo-European language family, Germanic branch, West Germanic subgroup, with origins in the medieval period and continuing into the present.

North American English is the source language for terms categorized here by language, reflecting vocabulary that has been adapted or borrowed into other languages. The category groups terms derived from this variety, illustrating cross-linguistic influence within the broader English family.

American English is the form of English spoken in the United States. It is a living language with a distinctive vocabulary and spelling that has given rise to many terms later borrowed by other languages.

Middle English is the historical stage of the English language spoken in England and parts of Britain from roughly the late 12th to the late 15th century. It belongs to the Indo-European language family, Germanic branch, West Germanic group, with English as its descendant.

Northern Middle English is a historical form of English used in northern England during the medieval period. It is categorized as a language variant whose vocabulary and terms have influenced later English dialects. The name reflects its geographic and temporal scope within the broader Middle English stage.

Esperanto is a constructed international auxiliary language designed to foster cross-cultural communication. It was created in the late 19th century by L. L. Zamenhof in Europe, with a simplified grammar and vocabulary drawn from several European languages, intended as a neutral second language.

Spanish is a Romance language in the Indo-European family, developed from Vulgar Latin in the Iberian Peninsula during the Middle Ages. It is spoken primarily in Spain and Latin America, with widespread global use and status as a major international language.

Early Modern Spanish is a historical stage of the Spanish language. Its vocabulary has contributed words to various languages, as shown by etymology categories that trace terms derived from Early Modern Spanish across languages globally.

Lunfardo is a slang variety of Spanish that developed in Argentina, especially Buenos Aires, during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. It features a specialized lexicon drawn from Italian and other sources and is used as a social cant rather than a separate standard language.

Category: Terms derived from Mexican Spanish by language catalogs loanwords that have entered other languages from Mexican Spanish. It records lexical items attributed to Mexican Spanish and traced into other languages, illustrating how Mexican Spanish has contributed vocabulary to diverse linguistic families.

Philippine Spanish is a Romance language variety of Spanish spoken in the Philippines. It has contributed loanwords and calques to local languages, reflecting its colonial-era influence that persists in some communities today and remains a focus of etymological study.

United States Spanish is the source language for a set of terms that have been borrowed into other languages. It is documented in a category of terms derived from United States Spanish by language, highlighting lexical items that originated in this variety and spread into neighboring language communities.

Yup'ik is a language in the Yupik language family. It is categorized as Yup'ik language, illustrating its place within this linguistic branch and its documentation in reference resources. The designation helps researchers compare its vocabulary and structure with related Yupik languages.

Proto-Eskimo is a reconstructed proto-language believed to be the common ancestor of the Eskimo languages within the Eskimo-Aleut family. It is not directly attested and its features are inferred from comparison of descendant languages.

Inuktun is an Inuit language belonging to the Inuit language family of the Arctic. It is associated with Inuit communities and is listed as a member of the Inuit languages in linguistic references, reflecting its role in Arctic communication and cultural heritage.

Proto-Eskimo-Aleut is a reconstructed proto-language and not directly attested. It represents the hypothetical ancestor of the Eskimo-Aleut language family, from which the Eskimo and Inuit languages descend, as inferred through comparative linguistic methods by scholars.

Estonian is the language listed under the Estonian language category of a lexical reference. It is treated as a distinct language entry within that category, grouping lemmas and forms by language name for organized access.
Etruscan is an ancient language of Italy, known from inscriptions dating to the early first millennium BCE. It is attested in central Italy and was used by the Etruscan civilization.

Basque is a language isolate spoken in the Basque Country of northern Spain and southwestern France. It has a documented history in the region and is not known to belong to any established language family.

Proto-Basque is the reconstructed ancestor of the Basque language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative data on Basque varieties, with its regional association tied to the western Pyrenees, though exact location and date remain unattested.

Even is a human language categorized under the Even language group. Linguistic resources describe it as a distinct language with its own morphological and syntactic features within the broader Tungusic language family. It has been studied in historical and descriptive linguistics, contributing to understanding of Eurasian language contact and variation.

Evenki is a human language categorized under the Evenki language. The available material provides no explicit details on its status, linguistic family, geographic region, or historical period. It is listed among linguistic entries without claims about current usage or speaker numbers.

Extremaduran is a Romance language spoken in the Extremadura region of Spain. It forms part of the Ibero-Romance group within Western Romance and is treated in some sources as a distinct language rather than merely a Spanish dialect.

Persian is an Iranian language of the Indo-Iranian branch, descended from Old Persian and Middle Persian and known in its modern form as New Persian. It is spoken in Iran, Afghanistan, and Tajikistan, with regional varieties such as Farsi, Dari, and Tajik, dating from around the medieval period to the present.

Classical Persian is an Iranian language in the Indo-Iranian branch, historically used as the literary standard of Persian. It has contributed vocabulary to many other languages through loanwords and terminology, with terms derived from Classical Persian recorded across various language families.

Early New Persian is a stage of the Persian language within the Iranian language family. It was historically used in Iran and surrounding regions during the medieval period and served as the basis for later varieties of Persian; many terms in other languages derive from its vocabulary.

Iranian Persian is a Western Iranian language in the Indo-Iranian branch of the Indo-European family. It has contributed vocabulary to other languages, as shown by terms derived from Iranian Persian by language, reflecting historical contact among Iranic-speaking communities.
Annobonese is a language categorized as Annobonese language. It is associated with the Annobón region and is used as a linguistic label in reference works.

Fala is a language with a dedicated category listing its name. The available material does not provide details on its classification within a language family, regional origin, or historical period. Any assertion about its usage or speakers would require direct sources beyond this listing.

West Miraya Bikol is a Bikol language of the Philippines, part of the Austronesian language family. The entry is listed as a language category, suggesting it is a named language within the Bikol subgroup, but specific speaker numbers, status, official recognition, or an approximate historical period are not provided.

Fula is a living Niger-Congo language of West Africa with multiple varieties spoken across a wide geographic area. It belongs to the Atlantic-Congo branch and is part of the wider Fula-Sara group within the Niger-Congo language family.

Finnish is a Finnic language in the Uralic language family. It is spoken in Finland and in nearby regions, with a long-standing literary and cultural presence in education and media. It is a modern, living language with official status in Finland.

Meänkieli is a Finnic language of the Uralic language family. It is spoken in the Torne Valley region of northern Sweden and nearby parts of Finland and is a living language closely related to Finnish.

Proto-Finno-Ugric is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Finno-Ugric branch of the Uralic language family. It is reconstructed and not directly attested; its form is inferred from comparison of descendant languages to explain their shared features.

Fijian is a language of Fiji. This designation appears in linguistic catalogs as the proper name for the language spoken by communities in the Fiji islands, providing a simple, stable label for reference in studies of regional linguistic variation and history.

Kven is a Finnic language in the Uralic language family spoken in Northern Norway by the Kven people. It is closely related to Finnish and Meänkieli and exists as a minority language with limited intergenerational transmission in a contemporary regional context.

Fanagalo is a pidgin and creole language of Southern Africa. It is derived primarily from Zulu and English, reflecting contact between diverse speakers in multilingual settings. The language has been used in regional work and social contexts, particularly in urban and mining areas, where speakers adopt a simplified, mixed vocabulary and grammar to facilitate communication.

Faroese is a North Germanic language spoken in the Faroe Islands. It is part of the Indo-European language family and descended from Old Norse, with its own literary tradition since the medieval period. The language has its own standardized orthography and is used in education and media within the islands.

Fon is a West African language of the Niger-Congo family. It is represented as a distinct linguistic system with its own vocabulary and grammar, reflecting its historical development within the region.

Pichinglis is a human language named Pichinglis and identified in linguistic reference materials by its corresponding language category. Current information does not specify a broader language family, regional homeland, or historical period, and there is no direct attestation beyond its categorization. The entry presents a straightforward description without additional ethnographic or sociolinguistic detail.

French language is the subject indicated by the category French_language. This label groups related terms and etymology-focused data under a single linguistic entry. The label serves to organize cognates, derivatives, and historical forms for etymological study.

Canadian French is a variety of the French language spoken in Canada. It belongs to the Romance group within the Indo-European language family and includes regional varieties across provinces and communities. The category focuses on terms derived from Canadian French by other languages, illustrating lexical influence rather than a separate historical proto-language.

Louisiana French is a Romance language variety spoken in Louisiana, United States. It is part of the French language family within Indo-European and developed from colonial-era French in North America; it remains a regional language with ongoing use in communities, literature, and media from the colonial period onward.

Cajun French is a French-based language variety with its own distinct vocabulary. It has contributed lexical items to other languages, evidenced by a category of terms derived from Cajun French by language, which helps etymologists trace cross-language influence and historical contact.

Frankish is a historical language whose lexicon has contributed terms to other languages, as reflected by a category of terms derived from Frankish by language. This indicates its lexical influence across European languages in historical contact contexts. It is evidenced by loanwords rather than a living speech community.

Middle French is a historical stage of the French language, belonging to the Romance family within the Gallo-Romance branch. It was spoken in medieval and early modern France, roughly from the 14th to the 17th century.
Old French is a historical stage of the French language, part of the Romance language family, within the Gallo-Romance branch. It was spoken in what is now France during the medieval period.
Old Northern French is a historical Romance language variety that forms a precursor to modern French. It was used in northern France during the medieval period.

Picard Old French is a medieval form of the Old French language, belonging to the Romance language family. It is associated with the Picardy region in northern France, and its vocabulary has yielded terms borrowed into other languages, as documented in linguistic derivations.

Franco-Provençal is a Romance language of the Gallo-Romance branch. It is tied to the historical Franco-Provençal linguistic area spanning eastern France, western Switzerland, and northwestern Italy. This description derives from category data and does not include contemporary usage specifics.

North Frisian is a member of the Frisian language group within the West Germanic branch of the Indo-European family. It is spoken in the North Frisian region along the coast of northern Germany and reflects a long regional linguistic tradition with several distinct dialects.

East Futuna is a language named for the East Futuna region. The available material provides limited detail on its linguistic classification or historical period, so its family, status, and era cannot be confirmed from the cited source.

Friulian is a language identified by the name Friulian. It is listed in a category focused on the Friulian language, illustrating its presence in linguistic and etymological reference material. The entry serves as a data point for comparing word origins and derivations across related languages in linguistic studies.

Old Friulian is the historical form treated as the source of terms categorized by language. This category collects terms derived from Old Friulian by language, illustrating how this historical form has contributed to vocabulary across different linguistic traditions.

Fwe is a human language recorded in linguistic references. The entry is categorized under Fwe language, indicating its recognition as a distinct language in the region where Fwe is spoken. As with many lesser-documented languages, detailed information on its exact family affiliation, regional distribution, and historical period is limited in the source material.

West Frisian is a West Germanic language in the Frisian branch. It is spoken in the Friesland region of the Netherlands and has long-standing literary and spoken usage dating back to medieval times. It occupies a recognized regional language status in the Dutch context.

Irish is a living Celtic language in the Goidelic branch of the Insular Celtic subgroup, part of the Indo-European language family. It is principally spoken in Ireland and has historical roots in the Gaelic linguistic tradition.

Ga is a living Niger-Congo language of the Kwa branch spoken by the Ga people in southern coastal Ghana. It is part of the regional language family with attested use in both historical and contemporary community communication.

Gagauz is a Turkic language spoken by the Gagauz people in Moldova’s autonomous region of Gagauzia and surrounding areas. It belongs to the Turkic language family and has historical and cultural continuity in the region, with attested use in traditional communities.

Garhwali is an Indo-Aryan language spoken in the Garhwal region of northern India. It forms part of the wider North Indian language group and has a long history in regional speech and folklore, reflecting its deep regional roots and traditional usage.

Gbagyi is a living Niger-Congo language of central Nigeria spoken by the Gbagyi people in communities around the Jos Plateau and adjacent regions. It has a long regional history and is used in everyday communication and local cultural practices. The language continues to be learned by children and used in traditional and informal settings.

Antillean Creole is a French-based creole language of the Caribbean, part of the broader creole language family. It developed in colonial-era contact between French and West African languages and regional varieties, and is used in Caribbean communities as a means of daily communication.

Grenadian Creole English is an English-based creole language of Grenada. It is part of the Caribbean creole family and arose through contact between English and Grenadian languages, forming a distinct local variety used in Grenadian speech.

Guianese Creole is an English-based creole language of the Caribbean. It is associated with Guyana, where it is used as a community language alongside English and other varieties. Its classification as a Creole places it within the English-based Creole family and reflects its use in daily communication.

Scottish Gaelic is a Goidelic Celtic language spoken in Scotland. It belongs to the Celtic language family, Goidelic branch, and is used in the Highlands and Islands. The language has a documented history in Scotland and survives in traditional and modern speech communities.

Mehri is a language identified by the Mehri language category. It is treated as a distinct language with a dedicated entry within that classification, reflecting its recognition as a named language in the Mehri linguistic grouping.

Proto-Germanic is the reconstructed ancestor of the Germanic language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparisons among descendant languages such as Gothic, Old Norse, and Old High German within the Indo-European context.

Suevic is a language from which terms have been derived by other languages, as documented in linguistic listings. The page catalogs terms derived from Suevic by language, illustrating historical linguistic contact and the transfer of vocabulary. This entry treats Suevic as a source language for etymological study.

Ge'ez is a Semitic language in the Afroasiatic language family, part of the Ethio-Semitic branch. It historically functioned as the classical language of ancient Ethiopian kingdoms and today survives mainly as a liturgical language in the Ethiopian and Eritrean Orthodox traditions.

Classical Gaelic is the historical form of the Gaelic language, a member of the Goidelic branch of the Celtic languages. It was used in medieval Ireland and Scotland and forms the literary standard of that period.

Gilbertese is an Austronesian language spoken in the Gilbert Islands, part of Kiribati in the central Pacific. It belongs to the Oceanic branch of the Austronesian family and has historical development linked to other Micronesian and Polynesian languages.

Hinukh is a human language identified by a dedicated Hinukh_language entry. It is presented as a distinct language with its own basic vocabulary and terminology, though detailed classification, regional history, and speaker information are not provided in this brief description.

Gojri is a language entry listed under the Gojri language category. It represents a human language with lexical items and grammatical evidence collected for etymological study. The name Gojri denotes a distinct linguistic tradition, documented for comparative analysis and historical linguistics.

Byzantine Greek is a historical variety of the Greek language spoken in the Byzantine Empire from late antiquity into the late medieval period. It evolved from Koine Greek and was used in administration, liturgy, and literature, leaving a legacy visible in later Greek dialects and standard forms.
Galician is a language documented in a dedicated Galician language category. It appears within a collection of language resources that cover vocabulary, etymology, and usage related to Galician.

Northwest Pashayi is an Iranic language of the Indo-Iranian language family, classified within the Pashayi branch. It is spoken in Afghanistan by communities associated with the Northwest Pashayi varieties and is recorded as a distinct member of the Pashayi languages.
Gilaki is an Iranian language of the Northwestern branch spoken in Gilan province along the Caspian Sea in Iran. It is a living language in the Iranian family with a long history in the region.

Crimean Gothic is an extinct East Germanic language of the Gothic branch, once spoken by Crimean Goths in the Crimea. It is part of the Indo-European family and is attested in early modern manuscripts dating to the 16th-18th centuries, with no surviving native speakers, and it reflects Gothic heritage in a Crimean context.

Middle High German is a historical stage of the German language, part of the West Germanic branch of the Indo-European family. It was spoken in the central and southern German-speaking areas of Europe during the High Middle Ages, roughly between the 11th and 14th centuries.

Middle Low German is a historical stage of the Low German language, within the West Germanic branch of the Indo-European language family. It was used in northern Germany and parts of the Netherlands from roughly the 11th to the 15th century, preceding Early New High German and contributing to regional medieval literary and religious texts.

Gutnish is a language entry categorized under Gutnish language. It appears in a category that groups related terms and linguistic data for the language. The entry serves as a reference point for vocabulary and etymology within this language.
Jamtish is a language name used in lexicographic references. It is listed under the Jamtish_language category, with recorded words and etymologies gathered in related linguistic resources.

Middle Norwegian is a medieval stage of the Norwegian language, part of the North Germanic language family. It was spoken in Norway during the Middle Ages and is known from historical records and texts of that period. The form represents a transitional phase between Old Norse and later Norwegian varieties, with features reflecting dialectal diversity across Norwegian regions.

Old Danish is an historical form of the Danish language, belonging to the North Germanic branch of the Indo-European family. It was used in what is now Denmark and neighboring regions during the medieval period.

Old Gutnish is a historical North Germanic language of Gotland, Sweden. It represents the medieval stage of the Gutnish language and is known from medieval sources and inscriptions. It is not a living language and is studied primarily through linguistic and historical records.

Old Swedish is the medieval stage of the Swedish language, a North Germanic language of the East Norse branch spoken in Sweden during the Middle Ages, roughly from the 13th to the early 16th century. It is the historical ancestor of Modern Swedish.

Late Old Swedish is a historical stage of the Swedish language, within the North Germanic branch of the Indo-European language family. It was spoken in medieval Sweden and neighboring regions as the language evolved toward Early Modern Swedish, providing the linguistic groundwork for later Standard Swedish.

Proto-Norse is the reconstructed ancestor of the North Germanic languages. It is not directly attested; researchers reconstruct it by comparing later Norse dialects and other North Germanic varieties to explain shared phonological and lexical features.

Scanian is a language categorized under the Scanian language category. The entry provides a catalogued term with related terms in the same group, illustrating its organization within a broader language-category framework. This description remains neutral and factual, without claiming current usage or speakers.

Proto-Anglo-Frisian is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Anglo-Frisian subgroup within the West Germanic branch of the Germanic language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative evidence among related languages such as English and Frisian.

Central Franconian is a West Central German language variety spoken in parts of central Franconia in Germany. It belongs to the West Central German branch of the Germanic language family and encompasses regional dialects used historically and in modern times.

East Central German is a West Germanic language in the High German group, part of the Central German language continuum. It is spoken in eastern parts of Central Germany, including regions such as Saxony and Thuringia, reflecting historical development within the German language family.

Fingallian is an extinct Celtic language of the Insular Celtic family, related to Irish; it was historically spoken in the Fingal region (around Dublin) during the late medieval to early modern period by various communities.

Gottscheerish is a historic Germanic language of the Gottschee German community in present-day Slovenia. It is classified within the Germanic branch of the Indo-European language family and is associated with the Gottschee region's historical settlement; it is not a contemporary lingua franca and is mainly documented in historical sources.

Jersey Dutch is a West Germanic language in the Dutch family, associated with the island of Jersey in the Channel Islands. It reflects historical use of Dutch-related speech on Jersey and contributes to the linguistic history of the region.

Middle Scots is a historical form of the Scots language used in Scotland during the medieval and early modern periods. It is known from literary and documentary sources and represents an older stage in the development of Scots, preceding modern Scots varieties.

Proto-North Sea Germanic is a reconstructed proto-language of the Germanic family, inferred as the common ancestor of North Sea Germanic languages such as English, Frisian, and Old Saxon. It is not directly attested and is reconstructed from descendant languages spoken around the North Sea region in the early first millennium BCE to early first millennium CE.

Proto-West Germanic is a reconstructed ancestor of the West Germanic languages, forming part of the Germanic branch of the Indo-European family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from later Germanic languages such as Old High German and Old English.

Rhine Franconian is a West Central German language variety of the West Germanic branch, spoken in the Rhine region of western Germany and parts of neighboring areas. It developed during the medieval period as a set of dialects rather than a single standard, with ongoing use in regional speech.

Sathmar Swabian is a Germanic language variety in the Swabian branch. It is associated with the Sathmar/Szatmár region and Danube Swabian communities. It forms part of the broader Germanic language family and reflects historical settlement patterns in Central Europe.

Transylvanian Saxon is a Germanic language spoken by the Transylvanian Saxons in Transylvania, Romania. It belongs to the West Germanic branch of the Indo-European language family and has historical use in the region from the medieval period to the present.

Volga German is a Germanic language in the Indo-European family associated with the Volga German community along the Volga region of Russia. It is documented primarily in historical or lexical sources rather than as a current standard language.

Zipser German is a Germanic language variety associated with the Zipser Germans in the Spiš region. It is categorized as a German language variety within the West Germanic group and is attested in historical and regional contexts, reflecting a localized form of German used by a particular community.

Mycenaean Greek is an extinct form of the Greek language, known from Bronze Age inscriptions written in Linear B. It belongs to the Indo-European language family, Hellenic branch, and was used in mainland Greece and Crete roughly between 1600 and 1100 BCE.

Classical Guarani is a historical stage of the Guarani language, a Tupian language in the Guarani subgroup. The name signals its identification as a classical or archaic form within linguistic classifications, distinct from modern everyday Guarani varieties.

Guanche is an extinct Afro-Asiatic Berber language formerly spoken in the Canary Islands. It belonged to the Berber branch of Afro-Asiatic and was spoken by the Guanche people on the Canary Islands. The language disappeared after European contact in the 15th century.

Old High German is a historical stage of the West Germanic language family, attested in early medieval texts in the German-speaking regions of central Europe. It is a precursor to Middle High German and to modern Standard German within the Germanic branch.

Gondi is a Dravidian language of central India, classified within the Dravidian language family. It is associated with the Gondi people and spoken across regions of central India, reflecting historical contact and regional diversity within the central Indian linguistic landscape. Its varieties show long-standing use in the local communities.

Gorontalo is an Austronesian language spoken in Gorontalo Province, on the island of Sulawesi, Indonesia. It is part of the Malayo-Polynesian branch within the Austronesian family, documented in linguistic references that categorize Gorontalo among regional languages.

Gothic is an extinct language of the East Germanic branch of the Germanic language family. It is known from late antique texts and inscriptions, representing a historical stage of the Germanic languages. Its geographic distribution tied to Gothic communities in parts of eastern Europe and the Balkans during late antiquity.

Guiqiong is a human language represented in a category devoted to Guiqiong language. It is documented for linguistic reference and comparison within that category, serving as a label for studies of its vocabulary, phonology, and morphology in the context of related languages.

Chichewa is a Bantu language in the Niger-Congo language family. It forms part of the Chichewa language group and has a long-standing literary and oral tradition in its traditional speech communities across southeastern Africa.

Nzadi is a language identified in a linguistic category entry. The available material notes its designation as Nzadi language without providing details on its status, linguistic family, region, or historical period. Further specifics such as scripts, dialects, or contact history are not included.

Old Avar is a historical form of the Avar language within the Northeast Caucasian language family. It is known from historical materials and represents an earlier stage of the Avar–Andic subgroup in the Caucasus region, with limited documentation and no direct modern descendants.

Old Burmese is a historical stage of the Burmese language, part of the Sino-Tibetan language family, specifically the Tibeto-Burman branch. It was historically spoken in the region of Burma (Myanmar) during the medieval to early modern periods.

Old Breton is an early form of the Breton language, belonging to the Brittonic branch of the Celtic language family, historically spoken in Brittany. It represents an older stage of the language before its later medieval and modern forms.

Occitan is a Romance language of the Indo-European language family. It is spoken in southern France and in parts of Italy and Spain, where it has historical regional use and contemporary communities. It originated from Vulgar Latin as part of the Western Romance branch, forming a distinct Occitan linguistic tradition.

Gascon is a language from which terms have been borrowed into other languages, as indicated by a category listing words derived from Gascon by language. The entry highlights Gascon's role in historical lexicon development and cross-language borrowing, illustrating how lexical items originating in Gascon appear across different linguistic contexts.

Provençal is a Romance language in the Occitan branch, traditionally spoken in the Provence region of southeastern France. It has given rise to many terms that have entered other languages, reflecting its historical influence on vocabulary across language groups.

Old Provençal is a historical Romance language of the Occitan group, spoken in medieval Provence in southern France. It represents an early form of Provençal within the Occitan linguistic continuum, dating to the medieval period and reflecting regional Provençal dialects of that era.

Old Chinese is the ancient stage of the Chinese language. It is attested in early inscriptions and historical texts, serving as the ancestor of later Chinese varieties, and reflecting historical sound changes, lexical developments, and grammatical patterns observed in later stages.

Old Cornish is a historical form of the Cornish language and is categorized within the Old Cornish language category. It represents an early stage of a Brittonic Celtic language once spoken in Cornwall, in the southwest of Great Britain, dating to the medieval period.

Old Dutch is a West Germanic language historically spoken in the Low Countries, including what is now the Netherlands and parts of Belgium, during the early medieval period. It is the historical ancestor of modern Dutch, attested in texts from roughly the 6th to the 12th centuries and preceding Middle Dutch.

Old Frisian is the historical form of the Frisian language. It is studied as an early stage of Frisian within the West Germanic language group and is known from historical records; scholars examine its vocabulary and grammar to understand the development of Frisian across northern Europe.

Old Georgian is a historical form of the Georgian language belonging to the Kartvelian family. It was spoken in the region of present-day Georgia in the Caucasus and is attested as an early stage of Georgian, providing the historical groundwork for later Georgian varieties.

Old Hungarian is a historical variety of the Hungarian language. It is represented in early written records and linguistic studies; scholars examine its features to understand historical development of Hungarian.

Ojibwe is a language categorized under the Ojibwe language category. It is associated with the language traditions and vocabulary of its speakers and appears in lexical resources that document forms, meanings, and usage patterns.

Old Japanese is the historical stage of the language labeled Old Japanese. It is a linguistic category used to group earlier texts and forms of the language as studied in historical and comparative contexts. This entry describes its status as a historical phase rather than a contemporary means of communication.

Western Ojibwa is a language in the Ojibwe branch of the Algonquian language family. It is associated with Western Ojibwa communities in North America, particularly around the Great Lakes region, and is documented in lexical resources as part of the Anishinaabe language continuum.

Middle Korean is a historical stage of the Korean language. It belongs to the Koreanic language family and was used on the Korean peninsula, with features documented in historical sources and later forms developing into modern Korean.

Old Korean is the historical form of the Korean language. It is referenced in linguistic category listings as Old Korean language, illustrating an earlier stage in the language's development. Scholarly work uses this label to distinguish earlier dialectal features and vocabulary from modern Korean.

Old Khmer is the historical stage of the Khmer language, part of the Austroasiatic language family. It is attested in inscriptions from historic Cambodia and dates to the medieval period. This form is studied as an ancient precursor to modern Khmer and is not a currently spoken everyday language.

Terms derived from Angkorian Old Khmer by language form a category that lists lexical items borrowed or inherited from the historical Khmer stage across various languages. The Angkorian Old Khmer entry represents a historical form of Khmer used in Southeast Asia, influencing neighboring languages through loanwords and shared roots.

Pre-Angkorian Old Khmer is a historical stage of the Khmer language in the Austroasiatic family, associated with the region of present-day Cambodia before the Angkor period. It is attested through later Khmer forms and inscriptions and provides the source of terms later derived into the Khmer lexicon.
Livvi is a Finnic language of the Uralic language family. It is spoken in parts of Karelia and nearby regions, with historical and contemporary use in areas of Finland and Russia.

Old Lithuanian is a historical form of the Lithuanian language, attested in older records from the Lithuanian region. It marks an early stage before later standardization and modernization of Lithuanian. Scholars rely on textual sources and linguistic features rather than direct native speech.

Oromo is a language identified by the name Oromo. The description signals the existence of a language by this name and situates it within lexical reference material without asserting regional, historical, or family details beyond the label itself.

Mochica is an extinct language of the Chimuan language family that was spoken in northern Peru. It is known from historical and linguistic sources rather than living speakers, and it forms part of the region’s ancient linguistic landscape as recorded in scholarly references.

Omok is a language listed under the Omok language category. The available entry does not provide explicit details on its language family, region, or period, so any further classification would be speculative. It is treated as a human language in linguistic references.

Old Manipuri is a historical stage of the Manipuri (Meitei) language spoken in the Manipur region. It is part of the Sino-Tibetan language family and is known from ancient inscriptions and literary texts dating to early periods of Manipuri.

Proto-Mixtecan is the reconstructed ancestor of the Mixtecan language family, not directly attested and inferred from comparative study of descendant Mixtecan languages across Mesoamerica, using shared phonological and lexical correspondences to reconstruct likely features.

Proto-Oto-Pamean is a reconstructed common ancestor of the Oto-Pamean branch of the Oto-Manguean language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from descendant languages spoken in regions of present-day Mexico, with estimates placing its use in prehistoric times before significant post-contact documentation.

Proto-Oto-Manguean is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Oto-Manguean language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from similarities among its descendant languages, providing a baseline for comparative historical linguistics and reconstruction practice.

Teojomulco Chatino is a language of the Chatino branch of the Oto-Manguean language family. It is spoken in the Teojomulco area of Oaxaca, Mexico, and is one of several related varieties within Chatino, a language family spoken by communities in southern Mexico.

Proto-Zapotecan is the reconstructed ancestor of the Zapotecan language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative data among Zapotecan languages. It represents a prehistoric stage in the development of Zapotecan languages.

Old Marathi is an early form of the Marathi language, belonging to the Indo-Aryan branch of the Indo-European language family, historically spoken in the region of present-day Maharashtra. It is studied for its historical development and linguistic features that shaped later Marathi.

Proto-Omotic is a reconstructed proto-language and is not directly attested. It is the hypothesized common ancestor of the Omotic language family, inferred from comparative evidence among its descendants. It is not directly attested in written records, and its exact geographic or temporal placement remains unresolved.

Old Mon is a historical stage of the Mon language, an Austroasiatic language of the Mon-Khmer branch once spoken in the Mon-dominated regions of present-day Myanmar and parts of Thailand. It represents an ancestral stage within the Mon linguistic lineage, preceding the later Mon varieties.

Oneida is a language of the Oneida people in North America. It is documented in linguistic resources under the Oneida language category, reflecting its historical use and ongoing study within the language family.

O'odham is a human language categorized under O'odham language in etymology references. It is documented for examining word origins, historical development, and related forms, providing insights into how vocabulary has evolved within this language.

Old Ossetic is an ancient stage of the Ossetian language. It provides a historical source of terms that appear in other languages through loan formation and derivation, reflecting linguistic contact and vocabulary transmission across regional languages.

Odia is an Indo-Aryan language of the Indo-European language family spoken in the Indian state of Odisha. It has a long attested literary history in that region and is categorized among the eastern group of Indo-Aryan languages.

Ormuri is a human language identified by linguistic references. It is referenced within a dedicated language category as Ormuri language, indicating recognition as a distinct language within scholarly resources. Scholars study its vocabulary, morphology, and historical connections within the broader study of language.

Old East Slavic is a historical language within the East Slavic group. It represents an early form of the East Slavic language family and is documented in linguistic research as part of the lineage leading to later languages in the region.

Ossetian is an Iranian language in the Indo-European family. It is spoken in the Caucasus by the Ossetian people and is part of the Eastern Iranian subgroup within Indo-European. Its documentation in historical and modern sources traces its development from early Iranian varieties through medieval period texts.

Oscan is an extinct Italic language of the Osco-Umbrian subgroup spoken in ancient southern Italy, including Campania and Samnite regions. It belongs to the Indo-European family and is known from inscriptions dating from the first millennium BCE, with documentation primarily through epitaphs and inscriptions rather than direct speakers today.

Osing is an Austronesian language of East Java, Indonesia, classified within the Malayo-Polynesian subgroup and spoken by the Osing people in the Banyuwangi Regency. It is a regional language with ongoing intergenerational transmission and serves in local daily life and culture.

Old Sundanese is the historical form of the Sundanese language spoken in western Java. It belongs to the Austronesian language family and represents an early stage of Sundanese, dating to ancient or medieval periods in historical records and inscriptions.

Old Spanish is a historical stage of the Spanish language, part of the Romance language family descended from Latin. It developed in the Iberian Peninsula and surrounding areas, reflecting medieval linguistic changes that shaped the later modern Spanish varieties.

Old Saxon is a historical West Germanic language spoken in parts of what is now northern Germany during the early medieval period. It is the earliest attested phase of the Saxon languages in the region, known from inscriptions and ancient writings that illuminate its phonology, morphology, and vocabulary within a broader Germanic context.

Ottoman Turkish is a historical Turkic language. It was used in the Ottoman Empire and is associated with its administrative and literary traditions, reflecting extensive cultural contact across its region. It belongs to the Turkic language family and is studied for its historical forms and scripts.

Old Tibetan is a historical form of the Tibetan language used in the Tibetan region during the early medieval period. It belongs to the Tibetic branch of the Sino-Tibetan language family and is the earliest attested stage of Tibetan, preserving inscriptions and manuscripts from that era.

Mezquital Otomi is an Otomi language of the Oto-Manguean family spoken in central Mexico, primarily in the Mezquital Valley in Hidalgo. It is one of several regional Otomi varieties documented in linguistic classifications and provides insight into the diversity of Otomi phonology and vocabulary.

Old Turkic is a historical Turkic language, the earliest attested stage of the Turkic language family. It is known from early medieval inscriptions in Central Asia, representing the formative period of Turkic languages and serving as a key reference point for comparative Turkic studies.

Proto-Otomian is the reconstructed ancestor of the Otomian language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred through comparative analysis of its descendant Otomi varieties. The reconstruction relies on shared phonological, lexical, and grammatical correspondences across Otomi languages, forming a plausible prehistory for the Otomian branch.

Estado de México Otomi is an Otomi language variety spoken in the State of Mexico, central Mexico. It is part of the Oto-Montan language family and represents a regional Otomi variety used in that area, with related varieties in nearby states.

Old Tamil is the historical stage of the Tamil language, a member of the Dravidian language family. It is attested in ancient inscriptions and early literature from the Tamil region, used in what is now Tamil Nadu and parts of Sri Lanka, dating to antiquity.

Old Uyghur is a historical Turkic language of Central Asia. It forms part of the Turkic language family and is documented in medieval Uyghur texts and inscriptions. The form is not a living language today and is studied through comparative analysis of its descendants and related scripts.

Elfdalian is a North Germanic language spoken in Älvdalen, Sweden. It is associated with the Elfdalian community and forms part of the North Germanic language family, tracing its roots to Old Norse. It is documented in linguistic sources and used in local contexts.

Old Welsh is an early form of the Welsh language, a historical Brittonic language within the Celtic branch of Indo-European. It was spoken in medieval Wales, representing an early stage of Welsh rather than a modern community language.

Punjabi is an Indo-Aryan language of the Indo-European family spoken in the Punjab region of South Asia, including parts of India and Pakistan. It has a long literary tradition and is represented by several standardized varieties.

Pangasinan is an Austronesian language of the Malayo-Polynesian family spoken in the Philippines, primarily in Pangasinan province and nearby parts of Luzon. It contains regional varieties and is used in everyday communication, local media, and education within Pangasinan-speaking communities.

Middle Persian is an Iranian language of the Western Iranian group. It dates to the Middle Persian period, roughly from the 3rd to the 10th centuries CE, and was used in Iran in various literary and religious texts.

Late Middle Persian is a historical stage of the Persian language. The lexicon includes terms derived from Late Middle Persian across various languages, illustrating its influence on later linguistic developments.

Kapampangan is a language of the Philippines. It is a distinct human language named for the Kapampangan people and recorded in language catalogs. The entry reflects its status as a language with its own lexical and grammatical traditions, contributing to the linguistic diversity of the region.

Papiamentu is a Caribbean creole language spoken on the ABC islands of Aruba, Bonaire, and Curaçao. It arose from historical contact among European languages and local substrates and is used as a community language in these islands.

Palauan is an Austronesian language spoken in Palau, Micronesia. It belongs to the Oceanic subgroup within Malayo-Polynesian of the Austronesian family and is traditionally spoken by the Palauan people. The region is Palau, in the western Pacific. The language has regional varieties and uses in traditional and contemporary contexts.

Paraujano is an Arawakan language of northern South America. It is listed under the Paraujano language category and is part of the broader Arawakan language family. The entry describes its linguistic classification rather than speakers, locations, or timeframes, reflecting its status as a documented language in linguistic resources.

Bouyei is a language name categorized in a linguistic resource. It serves as a label for linguistic materials related to Bouyei, without asserting a specific status, language family, region, or historical period here. The entry supports identification and cross-referencing for etymology research.

Picard is a Romance language in the Langue d'oïl group of the Romance language family. It is historically associated with northern France and nearby regions, and it is recognized as a distinct language category under Picard language.

Duruwa is a language listed in the Duruwa language category. The available material provides no details on its status, language family, region, or historical period. It appears as a standalone language entry without attested references to speakers or specific geographic context.

Nigerian Pidgin is an English-lexified pidgin language spoken in Nigeria. It functions as a common means of communication among diverse linguistic communities in the region, bridging languages in urban and rural contexts.

Pennsylvania German is a West Germanic language in the Germanic language family. It is associated with the Pennsylvania region in the United States and descends from early German dialects brought to North America by settlers.

Plautdietsch is a language categorized under Plautdietsch language on Wiktionary. It is documented in the Wiktionary taxonomy as a language name under the Plautdietsch language category, indicating its status as a recognized language entry within this resource.

Kayan is a language categorized under the Kayan language category. Documentation highlights its basic linguistic structure and vocabulary within the Kayan language group, illustrating its place in the local linguistic landscape and its historical development within related language varieties.

Peranakan Indonesian is a language entry listed in a Wiktionary category. It designates a regional language variety labeled as Peranakan Indonesian, with the source providing no additional attested details about its status, lineage, region, or period; the category groups terms associated with this name.

Bonan is a language identified by the Bonan language category. It represents a distinct linguistic system associated with the Bonan people and is documented in linguistic resources that group related varieties under the Bonan language.

Old Persian is an ancient Iranian language of the Old Persian subgroup. It is a historical language from the Achaemenid period in ancient Persia (present-day Iran), dating roughly to the 6th to 4th centuries BCE.

Petjo is a language categorized under the Petjo language category. It includes lexical items and usage patterns showing contact influences in its formation, records historical development and borrowings, and notes simple morphological tendencies without asserting specific contemporary usage.

Palatine German is a German language variety. The category documents terms derived from Palatine German by language, illustrating lexical items that originate in this variety and have been traced into other languages. This entry reflects etymological connections rather than contemporary usage.

Juba Arabic is a language categorized as Juba Arabic language. The available reference points to a category for the Juba Arabic language, indicating its recognition as a distinct language variety within linguistic categorization. This entry supports etymology-oriented descriptions.

Gandhari is a Middle Indo-Aryan Prakrit language associated with ancient Gandhara, in parts of present-day northern Pakistan and eastern Afghanistan. It is attested from the 3rd century BCE to the 4th century CE and is known from early Buddhist manuscripts and Kharosthi inscriptions.

Primitive Irish is an extinct early form of the Irish language, part of the Goidelic branch of the Celtic family. It was spoken in Ireland in ancient times and represents the precursor to later Old Irish.

Paelignian is an extinct Italic language spoken by the Paeligni in ancient central Italy. It forms part of the Italic language family and is known from early inscriptions, with its use confined to historical periods in the central Apennine region.

Proto-Philippine is a reconstructed proto-language, the ancestor of the Philippine languages within the Austronesian family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparison of its descendant languages in the Philippines. Its time depth is approximate and based on linguistic reconstruction rather than written records.

Phake is a language spoken by the Phake people in Southeast Asia. It is classified within the Phake language category on Wiktionary and is not documented here with further genealogical details. It is a distinct human language.

Palula is an Indo-Aryan language in the Indo-European language family. It is associated with Palula-speaking communities in South Asia and forms part of the broader South Asian Indo-Aryan linguistic zone; its documentation appears in linguistic reference work that groups it within the Indo-Aryan branch.

Phoenician is a Semitic language of the Afro-Asiatic family historically spoken by the Phoenician city-states along the Levant coast from roughly the first millennium BCE. It influenced later scripts and inscriptions across Mediterranean ports, with evidence coming mainly from inscriptions and ancient texts.

Pali is a Middle Indo-Aryan language of the Indo-European family. It is historically associated with early Buddhist scriptures in the Indian subcontinent, including Sri Lanka, and is used mainly as a liturgical and scholarly language in Theravada Buddhist contexts.

Pitcairn-Norfolk is a language categorized under the Pitcairn-Norfolk language group. It is associated with the Pitcairn-Norfolk region in linguistic classifications, reflecting its membership in a small regional language category.

Powhatan is a historical language of the Powhatan people in the Virginia region, listed in Wiktionary under the Powhatan language. It is classified within the Algonquian language family and is known from historical sources rather than contemporary daily use.

Pijin is an English-based creole language spoken in the Solomon Islands. It arises from contact between English and local languages and serves as a regional lingua franca in urban and rural communities. The language is part of the English-based creole family and shows vocabulary and grammatical features drawn from English with local influence.

Pitjantjatjara is an Australian Aboriginal language in the Pama–Nyungan family, spoken by the Pitjantjatjara people in central Australia. It is a distinct language within that family, with documentation of its vocabulary and grammar in linguistic resources.

Pökoot is a living language spoken by the Pokot people. It is treated as a distinct language in linguistic reference works and categorized under the Pokot language, emphasizing its identity as a standalone language rather than a dialect and noting its documentation in linguistic categories.

Polish is a West Slavic language of the Indo-European family, associated with Poland. It has a long documented history in Central Europe and is used as the national language in Poland. Its classification and regional ties are reflected in linguistic resources and glossaries.

Palenquero is a Spanish-based creole language spoken in San Basilio de Palenque, Colombia. It belongs to Caribbean Spanish-based creole languages and traces its emergence to historical contact between Spanish and African linguistic groups during the colonial period.

Oluta Popoluca is a language. It is listed under the Oluta Popoluca language category, indicating a distinct linguistic unit in reference classifications used for cataloging and etymological study. The label serves as an identifier for organizing language data and related linguistic research.

Palaic is an extinct Indo-European language of the Anatolian branch, known from limited Bronze Age inscriptions in northwestern Anatolia. It is part of the ancient Anatolian language group related to Hittite and Luwian, reflecting an early stage of the Indo-European linguistic landscape in that region.

Brooke's Point Palawano is a Palawan language of the Austronesian family spoken in Brooke's Point, Palawan, Philippines. It belongs to the Palawan subgroup within Austronesian, with linguistic documentation focusing on its phonology, vocabulary, and grammar as part of regional Palawan languages.

Pamona is a language of Indonesia spoken by the Pamona people. It is an Austronesian language of the Sulawesi region and reflects linguistic features typical of regional languages in this area. Used in ordinary communication.

Sabir is a historical language categorized under the Sabir language category. It arose as a contact language in maritime Mediterranean settings, reflected in historical records and linguistic descriptions.

Piedmontese is a Romance language spoken in the Piedmont region of northwestern Italy. It is classified within the Lombard group of Gallo-Italic languages, reflecting its regional linguistic heritage. Usage varies by community, with traditional literature and local media contributing to its presence.

Old Piedmontese is a historical variety once spoken in the Piedmont region of northwestern Italy. The category lists terms derived from Old Piedmontese by language, highlighting its lexical legacy and cross-lingual derivations. It reflects how speakers influenced other vocabularies through time.

Tuamotuan is a Polynesian language within the Austronesian language family, categorized under the Oceanic subgroup and the wider Polynesian languages, associated with the Tuamotu archipelago region in French Polynesia. It forms part of the diverse Austronesian linguistic landscape of the Pacific.

Pontic Greek is a Greek language variety spoken by Pontic Greeks in the Pontus region along the Black Sea. It is part of the Hellenic branch of the Indo-European family and is historically attested from antiquity through later periods, with continued use in regional communities.

San Juan Atzingo Popoloca is a Popoloca language of the Oto-Manguean family spoken in Mexico. It is a distinct member of the Popolocan branch, with historical usage in the San Juan Atzingo region and documentation reflecting its linguistic heritage within the broader Popoloca languages.

Highland Popoluca is an Oto-Manguean language of the Popolocan branch spoken in the highlands of Mexico. It is a distinct language within its language family and is identified in linguistic resources as part of the Highland Popoluca group.

Pohnpeian is a language name used to identify the speech variety associated with the Pohnpean people. It is indexed in a category for Pohnpeian language on a public lexical reference, serving as a label for linguistic entries and related terms rather than a description of usage or speakers.

Sayula Popoluca is a language listed under a category named Sayula Popoluca language. The available material offers only the language name and category label, giving no explicit details about its classification, region, status, or historical period.

Guinea-Bissau Creole is a Portuguese-based creole language. It is spoken in Guinea-Bissau and forms part of the family of Atlantic West African creoles. It has a long-standing role as a lingua franca and marker of identity for Guinea-Bissau communities.

Polabian is an extinct West Slavic language once spoken by the Polabian Slavs along the Elbe in present-day eastern Germany. It forms part of the West Slavic branch of the Slavic language family and is known from historical records rather than contemporary usage.

Proto-Central-Eastern Malayo-Polynesian is a reconstructed proto-language that serves as the hypothesized common ancestor of Central-Eastern Malayo-Polynesian languages within the Malayo-Polynesian branch of the Austronesian family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative evidence among descendant languages.

Proto-Malayo-Chamic is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Malayo-Chamic languages, representing a hypothetical stage prior to their diversification. It is not directly attested, and its features are inferred from the comparison of descendant languages in that group.

Proto-Malayic is the reconstructed ancestor of the Malayic languages within the Austronesian family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparison of descendant languages such as Malay and Indonesian. The reconstruction situates Malayic in the Southeast Asia region, though exact dialectal areas or dates are not directly documented.

Proto-Malayo-Sumbawan is a reconstructed proto-language within the Austronesian language family, inferred as the common ancestor of Malayo-Sumbawan descendant languages. It is not directly attested and is established through comparative reconstruction of sound correspondences and shared vocabulary among its descendants.

Proto-Oceanic is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Oceanic languages, a major branch of the Austronesian language family. It is reconstructed and not directly attested, inferred from comparative evidence across descendant Oceanic languages, including phonology, lexicon, and grammar.

Proto-Eastern Polynesian is the reconstructed ancestor of the Eastern Polynesian language subgroup. It is not directly attested and is inferred through comparative reconstruction of descendant languages within the Polynesian family. Linguists reconstruct its phonology, lexicon, and grammar to explain shared innovations across Eastern Polynesian languages.

Proto-Nuclear Polynesian is a reconstructed ancestor of the Nuclear Polynesian languages, within the Polynesian branch of the Austronesian family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative evidence across descendant languages spoken in the Pacific, representing an early stage of Polynesian differentiation during the wider Austronesian expansion.

Proto-Polynesian is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Polynesian branch of the Austronesian language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparisons of descendant Polynesian languages spoken in the Pacific region of Polynesia.

Proto-Malayo-Polynesian is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Malayo-Polynesian languages within the Austronesian language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative evidence among descendant languages; it represents a reconstructed stage rather than a language with contemporary speakers.

Pipil is a Nahuan language of the Uto-Aztecan family, spoken by Pipil communities in El Salvador and parts of Guatemala. It is endangered, with a small number of fluent speakers and active revival efforts; ongoing documentation and teaching programs aim to preserve its vocabulary, grammar, and traditional forms within the region.

Proto-Eastern Malayo-Polynesian is the reconstructed ancestor of the Eastern Malayo-Polynesian branch of the Malayo-Polynesian language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparison of descendant languages across maritime Southeast Asia and the Pacific.

Malecite-Passamaquoddy is a language identified in linguistic references as a distinct language with a dedicated category name. It is treated as a language entry for lexical data and related linguistic information within its language grouping.

Prakrit is a historical language of the Middle Indo-Aryan branch in South Asia. It was used in ancient India and is the ancestor of later Indo-Aryan languages, continuing in various dialects through classical and medieval periods.
Khasa Prakrit is a Prakrit language of the Indo-Aryan family. It is a historical stage whose lexicon influenced terms in other languages, reflected in derivative terms categorized by language.

Magadhi Prakrit is an ancient Indo-Aryan language of the Prakrit group, historically spoken in the Magadha region of northern India. It is known from inscriptions and Buddhist texts dating to roughly the 5th century BCE to the 4th century CE, and it serves as a key stage in the development of later Prakrit and modern Indo-Aryan languages.

Maharastri Prakrit is a Prakrit language. It serves as the source language from which terms have been derived and documented in other languages, illustrating its historical role in shaping lexicon across linguistic traditions. This description remains high level and does not imply contemporary usage.

Niya Prakrit is a Prakrit language in the Indo-Aryan family. It is historically attested in ancient India as part of the Prakrit languages, a subgroup of Middle Indo-Aryan languages. The exact status, regional distribution, and dating specifics are not provided in the source entry.

Paisaci Prakrit is a Prakrit language in the Middle Indo-Aryan branch of the Indo-Aryan language family, associated with ancient India. It forms the basis for a set of terms derived from Paisaci Prakrit across various languages.

Proto-New Indo-Aryan is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Indo-Aryan languages within the Indo-European family. It is not directly attested; its features are inferred through comparative reconstruction from descendant languages, and the category of terms derived from Proto-New Indo-Aryan reflects its historical role in shaping the lexicon of the Indo-Aryan branch.

Sauraseni Prakrit is the source of a set of terms documented in a language-derivation category. This page collects terms that are derived from Sauraseni Prakrit by various languages, illustrating how vocabularies in descendant languages incorporate elements from this Prakrit stage.

Parachi is an Indo-Iranian language spoken in Pakistan. It belongs to the Northwestern Iranian branch of the Indo-Iranian family, reflecting its historical development among related languages in the region. As a standalone language, Parachi exhibits its own linguistic features and historical trajectory within the broader Iranian language landscape.

Principense is a language represented in the category labeled Principense language. The entry organizes lexical data within a structured taxonomy and links related terms for comparative etymology. This description emphasizes classification and etymological context rather than usage, speakers, or geographic distribution.

Old Prussian is an extinct Baltic language of the Indo-European family. It was spoken by the Old Prussians along the southeast Baltic coast in the Prussia region, with use ending by the early modern period.

Old Occitan is a medieval Romance language of the Occitano-Romance group, historically spoken in the Occitania region of southern France and surrounding areas during the Middle Ages. It is the historical predecessor of modern Occitan varieties and is studied through literary and documentary evidence from that period.

Perené Ashéninka is a language of the Ashéninka subgroup in the Amazon region. It is recognized as a distinct Perené Ashéninka language within regional language classifications and referenced in linguistic reference materials.

Pashto is a language categorized under the Pashto language category. This entry notes its listing in a linguistic resource and serves as a concise reference for etymology-related exploration, focusing on word origins and historical development within a broad language context.

Portuguese is a Romance language in the Indo-European family descended from Latin, developed on the western Iberian Peninsula. It is spoken in Portugal and Brazil and widely used in Africa and parts of Asia, with roots dating to the medieval period.

Brazilian Portuguese is the Brazilian variety of Portuguese, a Romance language spoken in Brazil and surrounding communities. It forms part of the Western Romance branch of the Indo-European language family and shows regional variation in pronunciation and vocabulary across the country.

Purepecha is a language. It is listed under the category Purepecha language on a lexical reference resource, indicating its recognition as a distinct language within language documentation projects.
