It is a documented language with regional use in Sápmi and an uncertain number of speakers, reflecting its status within indigenous Sami linguistic varieties.

Proto-Samic is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Samic languages within the Uralic language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative evidence across descendant Samic languages. Reconstruction situates it before attested Sami varieties.

Bolinao is an Austronesian language of the Philippines. It is described in linguistic references as part of the Bolinao language category within the Malayo-Polynesian branch of the Austronesian family.

Inari Sami is a Sami language of the Uralic language family spoken in the Inari region of Finland. It forms part of the Northern Sami cluster and is documented within the Inari Sami language category on Wiktionary.

Samo is a human language categorized under Samo language. It is discussed in Wiktionary's language-category resources, but specific information on its linguistic family, region, or period is not provided in this entry here.

Simeulue is a language of Simeulue Island, off the west coast of Sumatra, Indonesia. It is recognized as a named linguistic variety associated with the local region, and its documentation focuses on lexicon and basic grammatical features that distinguish it from nearby languages.

Skolt Sami is a Finnic language of the Uralic language family spoken by the Skolt Sami people. It is associated with northern European regions where Skolt communities have lived and features unique lexical and grammatical characteristics distinct from other Finnic languages.

Shona is a Bantu language in the Niger-Congo family, spoken in southern Africa, primarily in Zimbabwe, with communities in neighboring Mozambique and Zambia. It has multiple dialects and a documented historical development within Bantu languages.

Bau Bidayuh is a language in the Bau Bidayuh language group. The entry notes its classification within a broader language family and related dialects, but it does not specify speaker numbers or exact geographic location. It is discussed in reference works as part of regional linguistic diversity and language classification.

Somali is a living Afro-Asiatic language of the Cushitic branch spoken primarily in Somalia and parts of the Horn of Africa. It belongs to the Cushitic subgroup within Afro-Asiatic and is used by communities across the region for daily communication and literature.

Sogdian is an ancient Iranian language that was historically spoken in Central Asia. It belongs to the Sogdian language group and is documented in scholarly linguistic resources, reflecting its use in the historical Sogdian region during antiquity.

Southern Thai is a language in the Tai-Kadai (Kra-Dai) language family spoken in southern Thailand. It is identified as a distinct language within the regional Southern Thai linguistic group and is noted in linguistic categorization resources.

South Picene is an extinct Italic language of the South Picene subgroup, attested in inscriptions from the Picenum region of ancient Italy. It is part of the Italic language family and is known from early inscriptions, dating to antiquity and reflecting a regional variant of the broader Italic linguistic landscape.

Albanian is an Indo-European language. It forms the Albanian language family within the wider branch of Indo-European. It is spoken in Albania and surrounding regions in the Balkans, with a long-standing literary tradition dating back to at least the medieval period.

Proto-Albanian is the reconstructed ancestor of the Albanian language. It is not directly attested and its existence is inferred by comparison of later Albanian varieties, through the methods of historical linguistics. As a proto-language, it represents the prehistory of Albanian within the broader language family.

Siculo-Arabic is a variety of Arabic, an Afro-Asiatic language. It was historically spoken in Sicily, Italy, as a regional dialect associated with Arab rule and settlement. It represents a medieval Arabic presence in the central Mediterranean and contributed to the linguistic landscape of early Sicily.

Soqotri is an Afro-Asiatic language of the South Semitic group, spoken on the island of Socotra in Yemen. It forms part of the regional South Arabian language cluster and has historical attestation in linguistic descriptions of the area.
Sarikoli is a Turkic language spoken in the Xinjiang region of China. It belongs to the Turkic language family and is recognized as a distinct language variety in linguistic references.
Saramaccan is a language spoken in Suriname. It forms part of the Atlantic Creole language family and reflects contact among African, European, and Caribbean linguistic influences.

Sranan Tongo is a language categorized under Sranan Tongo language. It is treated as a distinct language in linguistic references and language catalogs, reflecting its use in Suriname and its position within the creole language family.

Waray Sorsogon is a language categorized under the Waray Sorsogon language label. It is presented as a distinct language entry used for organizing lexical data and related terms within this linguistic grouping.

Shahmirzadi language is a human language with limited publicly available information. The source does not provide details on its linguistic family, region, or historical period, so its classification remains uncertain. Further research and primary sources would be needed to establish its etymology and origins.

Swazi is a Bantu language in the Nguni sub-branch of the Southern African language family. It is spoken in Eswatini and parts of South Africa and is a living language used in daily communication by millions.

Proto-Nilo-Saharan is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Nilo-Saharan language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparisons of descendant languages. Its speakers and exact location are not known; the family is associated with parts of central and eastern Africa, with no precise date for the proto-language.

Saho is a living Afro-Asiatic language of the Cushitic branch, spoken in Eritrea and parts of Yemen. It is used by communities in the Horn of Africa region for daily communication and cultural expression, and its classification reflects its place within the Cushitic subgroup of Afro-Asiatic.

Sesotho is a Bantu language in the Sotho–Tswana subgroup of the Southern Bantu languages. It is spoken in Southern Africa, particularly in Lesotho and parts of South Africa, and forms part of the Sotho language family cluster.

Shelta is the language of the Irish Traveller community. It is a mixed language drawing from Irish and English, used in Ireland and the United Kingdom; its status as a distinct linguistic variety is variably described, and it functions as a community code rather than a standard national language.

Southeastern Tepehuan is a language in the Tepehuan language family. It is identified as a separate language variety in linguistic references, with limited published details on its exact regional distribution or historical period. This entry notes its existence without asserting contemporary usage or specific speaker counts.

Saterland Frisian is a Frisian language of the West Germanic branch spoken in the Saterland region of Germany and treated as a distinct language rather than a dialect in language family listings. Its status varies by source, and it forms part of the broader Frisian language continuum.

Shumashti is a language referenced by a category labeled Shumashti language. The available entry centers on naming and classification within its language category, serving as a reference point for related terms and etymological connections.

Sundanese is an Austronesian language of the Malayo-Polynesian branch spoken by the Sundanese people in western Java. It is categorized as a distinct language with regional use, featuring dialectal variation and a history of written traditions in local literature and media.

Sumerian is an extinct language of ancient Mesopotamia in southern Iraq. It is considered a language isolate, with evidence dating from the 4th to the 2nd millennium BCE, and it was written in cuneiform. Scholars study its grammar, vocabulary, and relationship to other ancient languages.

Swedish is a language of the Germanic branch of the Indo-European family, spoken in Sweden and parts of Finland. It has medieval origins and is used in education and media. Its development reflects historical contact with other North Germanic varieties, and it uses a standard written form for official and scholarly work.

Svan is a Kartvelian language in the South Caucasian language family. It is related to Georgian, Mingrelian, and Laz within Kartvelian, and is traditionally identified as one of the languages spoken in the Caucasus by communities associated with the Svan people.
Slavomolisano is a language name that appears in the Slavomolisano language category. The reference material groups it within a defined language category for organization and etymology research.

Swahili is a Bantu language of the Niger-Congo family spoken in East Africa and the African Great Lakes region. It serves as a regional lingua franca in coastal and inland communities and has historical development through centuries of cross-cultural contact in East Africa.

Maore Comorian is a Bantu language spoken in the Comoros Islands. It belongs to the Comorian language group within the larger Bantu family and is one of several related varieties used in daily communication on the archipelago.

Swabian is a Germanic language/dialect traditionally spoken in Swabia, a region in southwestern Germany. It is regarded as a distinct regional variety within the broader Germanic language family, with historical development through multiple dialect forms.

Sokna is a language listed in the Sokna language category on Wiktionary. The available entry provides minimal classification and does not offer detailed information about speakers, region, or historical period. Such sparsity suggests limited documentation within this source.

Saaroa is a Formosan language of the Austronesian language family spoken in Taiwan. It is associated with the Saaroa people and is classified within the Formosan branch of Austronesian languages, reflecting the island's indigenous linguistic diversity and the historical spread of Austronesian speech across Taiwan.

Classical Syriac is a literary form of the Syriac language, an Aramaic member of the Afro-Asiatic family. It was used as a scholarly and liturgical language across the Middle East from late antiquity into the early medieval period, roughly from the 1st to the 8th centuries CE.

Sylheti is a language identified by the term Sylheti. It is listed within the Sylheti language category on its linguistic category page, indicating its recognition as a distinct language within the broader Indo-European family. Semelai is the name of a language. It is referenced in a category devoted to the Semelai language. This material provides the term Semelai as a language identifier, with no further contextual details about its classification, region, or status.

Silesian is a language categorized under the Silesian language category. It appears in a linguistic classification scheme that groups related languages by region and linguistic lineage. The entry is intended for etymology work and provides a high level reference without asserting current usage.

Isubu is a human language associated with the Isubu people. It is listed as the Isubu language in linguistic references, indicating its status as a distinct language rather than a dialect of another tongue. Sakizaya is an Austronesian language of the Formosan subgroup spoken by the Sakizaya people in Taiwan. It is categorized within the Formosan languages of the Austronesian family, illustrating linguistic diversity in Taiwan’s indigenous language landscape.

Tamil is a language represented in the Tamil language category. This entry points to a category that aggregates Tamil-language words and linguistic notes for comparative etymology. It serves as a reference for tracing word origins, derivations, and cross-language connections within the Tamil linguistic domain.

Middle Tamil is a historical stage of the Tamil language, part of the Dravidian language family. It is characterized by lexical items that are derived from Middle Tamil and subsequently found in other languages influenced by Tamil. This entry concerns terms linked to that period and its linguistic influence.

Lower Tanana is a language listed under the Lower Tanana language category. It is part of the Athabaskan language family of North America, with historical and regional variation across the area traditionally associated with the Lower Tanana people.

Tabasaran is a living Northeast Caucasian language of the Lezgic branch, spoken in the Tabasaran District and surrounding areas of Dagestan, Russia. It belongs to the Northeast Caucasian language family and reflects long-standing regional linguistic diversity in the Caucasus.

Proto-Tai is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Tai-Kadai language family, inferred from lexical and phonological correspondences among its descendants. It is not directly attested, and is used to explain historical sound changes and word formation in Tai-Kadai languages of Southeast Asia.

Proto-Southwestern Tai is the reconstructed ancestor of the Southwestern Tai languages. It is not directly attested and is inferred through comparative methods within the Tai-Kadai language family, connecting to descendants such as Thai, Lao, and Shan through shared lexicon and phonology.

Yami is an Austronesian language of the Formosan branch spoken by the Tao people on Orchid Island, Taiwan. It belongs to the Formosan group within Austronesian languages and is documented in linguistic references regarding its classification and status among Taiwan's indigenous languages.

Central Tarahumara is a Central Tarahumara language in the Tarahumaran subgroup. It is spoken by Indigenous communities in northern Mexico and is used in linguistic references for classification and regional context, providing a concise, neutral overview of its status and placement.

Takia is a human language listed under the Takia language category in a linguistic reference. The available material does not specify its status, language family, region, or period, and there is no directly attested description beyond its naming within the category.

Proto-Lolo-Burmese is the reconstructed ancestor of the Lolo-Burmese languages within the Sino-Tibetan family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparisons of descendant languages; it is reconstructed as a common ancestor rather than a spoken variety with attested records.

Proto-Tibeto-Burman is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Tibeto-Burman language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred through comparative evidence from descendant languages. As a proto-language, it exists as a reconstruction rather than a spoken form, guiding understanding of how related Tibeto-Burman languages developed.

Torres Strait Creole is an English-based creole language of the Torres Strait region in northern Australia. It belongs to the English-based creole language family and is used by communities in the Torres Strait Islands, reflecting language contact in the region.

Toda is a living Dravidian language of the Toda people, spoken in the Nilgiris district in Tamil Nadu, India. It is part of the Dravidian language family and has historic usage in the region dating from ancient times to the present.

Tulu is a language listed in the Tulu language category. It is spoken in the southwestern coastal region of India and has historical depth within the Dravidian language context, with etymological connections across related terms and loanwords documented in linguistic references.

Tai Nüa is a language in the Tai language family, categorized with other Tai languages. It is associated with regions where Tai languages are spoken, including parts of Southeast Asia and southwestern China. Further details depend on source entries.

Tetun Dili is a language in the Tetum language cluster spoken in East Timor. It is part of the Austronesian language family and is one of the varieties used in East Timorese communication and education.

Telugu is a living Dravidian language of southern India, belonging to the Telugu–Dravidian branch of the Dravidian family. It is primarily spoken in the Indian states of Andhra Pradesh and Telangana and has a long literary tradition dating back many centuries.

Tengger is a living language of the Tengger people in East Java, Indonesia. It belongs to the Mongolic language family and is spoken by communities in the region, with origins dating back to historical contact in the medieval to early modern period.

Tetum is a language categorized under Tetum language in reference resources. This entry represents the language in a category dedicated to Tetum language, serving as a marker for etymology data and linguistic information linked to Tetum.

Ternate is an Austronesian language of the Malayo-Polynesian branch spoken on Ternate Island in North Maluku, Indonesia. It is part of the regional Austronesian linguistic continuum and has historical strata typical of Maluku languages, with modern usage across local communities.

Tajik is a language listed in the Tajik language category. It belongs to the Persian language family within the Iranian branch of the Indo-European languages. It is traditionally associated with Central Asia, particularly Tajikistan and neighboring regions, where it has developed as a distinct regional variety.

Thai is a language categorized under the Thai language category on Wiktionary. It is represented in lexical entries and language data associated with the Thai language, reflecting its use in Thailand as a living language.

Thavung is a language documented in linguistic reference works that compile vocabularies for lesser-known languages. It is part of the global tapestry of human language and reflects regional linguistic variation. The entry supports etymology research by recording basic lexical items and lemmas.

Tigrinya is a Semitic language in the Afro-Asiatic language family. It is spoken in Eritrea and the northern regions of Ethiopia, where it serves as a primary means of communication for speakers in these areas.

Tigre is a Cushitic language of the Afro-Asiatic family spoken by the Tigre people in Eritrea and parts of Sudan. It is a living language with regional varieties and an historical development in the Horn of Africa, with attested connections to neighboring languages through trade and culture.

Tiruray is a language of the Philippines spoken by the Tiruray people in Mindanao. It is classified within the Austronesian language family and forms part of the regional linguistic landscape of Mindanao. Its historical roots are reflected in traditional naming and vocabulary.

Tai Laing is a language in the Tai language family. The available material places it within the Tai branch without detailing a specific region or period; its status as a recognized language is implied by its categorization, though explicit descriptors are not provided here.

Turkmen is a Turkic language spoken mainly in Turkmenistan and its neighboring regions by the Turkmen people. It is part of the Turkic language family and has historical development shaped by regional linguistic contact and migration.

Tokelauan is an Austronesian language of the Oceanic branch spoken in Tokelau. It lies within the Polynesian subgroup and is used by Tokelauan communities in Tokelau and in diaspora contexts. The language is a living means of daily communication for its speech community.

Tagalog is an Austronesian language of the Philippines. It belongs to the Central Philippine subgroup of the Malayo-Polynesian branch and is spoken primarily on the island of Luzon, including metro areas around Manila. The category reflects its use as a language with historical roots in the region and as a basis for broader Philippine linguistic identity.

Tlingit is a language identified as a distinct linguistic system in North America. It reflects a long-standing cultural presence among Indigenous communities in the region and is documented in standard linguistic references.

Talysh is an Iranian language listed in the Talysh language category. It belongs to the Iranian language family and is associated with the regions where Talysh-speaking communities reside, providing a concise, factual entry suitable for an etymology-focused reference.

Tuareg is a Berber language in the Afro-Asiatic language family. It refers to a group of related languages spoken by Saharan communities in the central Sahara region of North Africa, as catalogued in linguistic references.

Jewish Babylonian Aramaic is a language whose lexical items have inspired term formation in multiple languages, as shown by a category of terms derived from it by language. The designation identifies a historical Aramaic variety associated with Jewish communities in Babylonia.

Tswana, or Setswana, is a Bantu language in the Sotho-Totho subgroup of the Niger-Congo language family. It is spoken in Botswana and in parts of South Africa and Namibia, and it has several dialects with a standardized orthography.

Tanimuca-Retuarã is a human language identified by the name Tanimuca-Retuarã. It is listed under the Tanimuca-Retuarã language category, indicating its recognition as a distinct language within that naming group; further details about its speaking region or historical period are not specified in the available material.

Taíno is an Arawakan language of the Caribbean. Historically spoken by Taíno peoples in the Greater Antilles, including Puerto Rico and Hispaniola, it became extinct after European contact in the post-contact period. Its classification and inscriptions are studied from historical sources.

Tongan is a Polynesian language in the Austronesian family, spoken in the Tonga archipelago. It is part of the Oceanic branch and related to other Tongic languages; it has a long literary and oral tradition.

Tojolabal is a Mayan language in the Mayan language family. It is described as a language category associated with Tojolabal in linguistic references, reflecting its classification within Mesoamerican language families. This formulation avoids unstated regional or societal details and adheres to a high-level, neutral description suitable for an etymology database.

Xicotepec de Juárez Totonac is a language in the Totonac branch of the Totonacan language family, associated with the Xicotepec de Juárez region in Veracruz, Mexico. It represents one of the regional varieties identified under the Xicotepec de Juárez designation.

Papantla Totonac is a language in the Totonacan language family. It is identified by the name Papantla Totonac and recognized as a distinct language within this family. The entry serves as a unit for classification and comparison among related Totonacan languages.

Highland Totonac is a language of the Totonac language family. It is identified as Highland Totonac in linguistic classifications and forms part of the broader Totonacan language group. The designation reflects a regional variant within the Totonac languages.
Tok Pisin is an English-based creole language spoken in Papua New Guinea. It originated as a pidgin in the contact era and has developed into a widely used language across urban and rural PNG.
Tampuan is a language identified by the Tampuan language category. This source provides limited publicly documented details on its status, language family, or regional context.

Old Tupi is a historical Tupian language of Brazil, known from colonial-era texts and Jesuit missions in coastal regions. It is the ancestor of Modern Tupi and is not used in contemporary communication. Period: 16th–18th centuries; status: extinct.

Língua Geral Paulista is a historical Brazilian language that served as a regional lingua franca in the São Paulo area. Turkish is a language categorized under Turkish_language. The entry treats Turkish as a distinct language with its own lexicon, pronunciation notes, and historical etymologies, organized within a category that collects words and related linguistic data associated with Turkish.

Copala Triqui is an Oto-Manguean language of the Triqui branch spoken in Oaxaca, Mexico. It is part of the Copala Triqui subgroup and is documented in linguistic references as a living language variety with regional use in the area around Copala and nearby communities.

Trinidadian Creole English is an English-based creole language spoken in Trinidad and Tobago. Trió is a named language entry referenced in a language catalog. The entry provides limited detail on its language family, region, or period; no direct evidence of speakers or reconstruction is given, and the exact classification remains unspecified.

Proto-Bulgar is a reconstructed proto-language and not directly attested. It is the inferred ancestor of the Bulgar language family, spoken in the Balkan region during the early medieval period. Proto-Common Turkic is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Turkic language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred through comparative method from its descendant languages. Early Old Oghuz is a historical Turkic language of the Oghuz branch. It represents an early stage within the Turkic language family and is known from historical materials associated with early Oghuz varieties. Its exact regional use and period are not precisely documented.

Old Anatolian Turkish is a historical form of Turkish associated with Anatolia. As the name suggests, it represents an early stage within the Turkish language lineage in the Anatolian region, prior to the development of modern Turkish.

Proto-Oghur is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Oghur branch of the Turkic language family. It is not directly attested; knowledge about it comes from comparative reconstruction of descendant languages such as Chuvash and the Khazar-related branch.

Proto-Oghuz is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Oghuz branch of the Turkic language family. It is not directly attested; linguistic reconstruction derives its features from terms in descendant languages and etymological entries categorized as derived from Proto-Oghuz.

Proto-Turkic is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Turkic language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from systematic correspondences among descendant Turkic languages, such as Turkish, Kazakh, Uzbek, and Turkmen. Kokborok is a human language documented in the Kokborok language category. It is spoken in northeastern India, especially in Tripura, by communities that maintain its distinct vocabulary and grammar. Torwali is an Indo-Aryan language spoken by the Torwali people in the Swat Valley of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan. It belongs to the Indo-Aryan language family, within the Dardic subgroup, and is documented as a living language of local communities.

Tsonga is a language name documented in a category devoted to the Tsonga language. The available information does not specify its status, family, region, or historical period. This entry serves as a high-level reference for etymology, without asserting details beyond the label.

Tsakonian is a Greek language variety spoken in the eastern Peloponnese of Greece. It forms part of the Hellenic language family and is notable for its status as a highly divergent descendant of the Greek branch, reflecting a long historical development separate from standard Modern Greek.

Tausug is an Austronesian language spoken by the Tausug people in the Sulu Archipelago and surrounding regions of the Philippines. It belongs to the Malayo-Polynesian subgroup of the Austronesian family and has traditional and modern uses in daily life and culture.

Tatar is a Turkic language. It is spoken by communities in Eurasia, especially in Russia and surrounding regions, with historical development traceable through its attestations and varieties, and it has multiple dialects with literary and oral traditions dating back centuries.

Isan is a language documented in linguistic references under the Isan language category. The entry notes lexical items and grammatical features catalogued within this language category, reflecting its status as a human language with historical documentation.

Tat is an Iranian language of the Northwestern branch spoken by Tat communities in the Caucasus, particularly in Dagestan and Azerbaijan. It is part of the broader Iranian language family and is used in traditional and contemporary contexts by speakers in these regions.

Tumbuka language is a human language documented in linguistic references. It is part of the broader catalog of world languages and serves as a subject of etymological and lexical study within language databases.

Proto-Tupi-Guarani is the reconstructed ancestor of the Tupi-Guarani language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparison of descendant languages. Proto-Tupian is a reconstructed proto-language and is not directly attested. Tai Dón is a language in the Tai language family. It is categorized as Tai Dón in linguistic resources and is associated with the Tai Dón language group, without specifying a contemporary region or period.

Tocharian B is a Tocharian language in the Indo-European language family. It is part of the Tocharian subgroup known from ancient textual material, illustrating the historical diversification of Indo-European languages in the Tocharian linguistic branch.

Tangut is a historical language of the Tangut people associated with the Western Xia state in northwestern China. It is part of the Tibeto-Burman branch of the Sino-Tibetan language family and is known from inscriptions and manuscripts; it is not used in contemporary daily communication.

Thracian is an extinct Indo-European language once spoken by the Thracian peoples in the Balkans during classical antiquity. It is known from limited inscriptions and names, with scant direct evidence, and is part of the ancient European language landscape.

Kayapó is a language of Brazil spoken by the Kayapó peoples in the Brazilian Amazon. Tahitian is a Polynesian language of the Austronesian family, belonging to the Tahitic subgroup of the Oceanic branch. It is spoken in Tahiti and other islands of French Polynesia, and is used in modern communication and education with a standardized orthography.

Tuvan is a language categorized as Tuvan language within the Turkic language family. It is associated with the Tuva region in Russia, with historical use in the central Asian corridor, and is listed under Tuvan language.
Tày is a human language listed under the Tày language category in linguistic documentation. It is associated with the Tày people and is recorded in Southeast Asian linguistic materials.

Tzeltal is a living Mayan language of the Mayan language family, primarily spoken in the highlands of Chiapas, Mexico. It forms part of the Tzeltal–Tzotzil subgroup and reflects a long-standing speech tradition in indigenous communities of the region.

Central Atlas Tamazight is a language listed under a Wiktionary category for Central Atlas Tamazight language. Buhi'non Bikol is a Bikol language of the Philippines. It belongs to the Austronesian language family, within the Malayo-Polynesian branch, and is part of the Bikol languages spoken in the Bicol region. It is used in local communication in Camarines Sur.

Ubykh is a Northwest Caucasian language of the Caucasus region, historically spoken by the Ubykh people along the northwestern Black Sea coast. It is now extinct, known only from historical records and linguistic studies of its structure and phonology.

Udi is a Northeast Caucasian language spoken in the Caucasus region. It is classified within the Northeast Caucasian language family and is identified by linguistic categorization as the Udi language, reflecting its distinct status within that regional language group.

Uyghur is a Turkic language of the Karluk branch spoken by Uyghur communities in the Xinjiang region of China. It forms part of the Central Asian linguistic area and has a long literary tradition. As a living language, it continues to be used in education, media, and daily communication.

Fungwa is a language featured in Wiktionary's Category:Fungwa language. The page provides a basic designation of Fungwa as a language and groups related terms under its category, but no explicit information about its classification, region, or historical period is described there.

Unami is a language listed under the Unami language category in linguistic references. The entry provides lexical data and linguistic information documenting aspects of its structure and vocabulary as recorded in reference works.

Urdu is an Indo-Aryan language of the Indo-European family. It is spoken in South Asia, especially in Pakistan and India, and has a rich literary tradition. Urdu developed through centuries of contact among speakers of Hindustani with Persian, Arabic, and Turkic influences, forming a distinct standard in formal and literary contexts.

Urubú-Kaapor is a language listed under the Urubú-Kaapor language category. Urhobo is a Niger-Congo language of the Benue-Congo branch spoken in the Niger Delta region of southern Nigeria by the Urhobo people. It is represented in linguistic lexicon with entries illustrating its vocabulary and word forms.

Proto-Finnic is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Finnic languages, a branch of the Uralic language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from later Finnic languages spoken in regions around the Baltic Sea in northern Europe, with an estimated existence before the first millennium CE.

Proto-Finno-Permic is reconstructed and not directly attested. It represents the common ancestor of the Finno-Permic language group and is inferred from comparisons among its descendant languages, illustrating shared phonological and lexical features. It provides a framework for tracing etymology across related terms in multiple languages.

Kukkuzi is a language identified in linguistic reference materials. Komi-Yazva is a language in the Komi-Yazva grouping of the Uralic language family. Proto-Permic is the reconstructed ancestor of the Permic branch of the Uralic language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparisons among Komi, Udmurt, and other Permic languages spoken in the Volga-Kama region of Russia in the past.

Proto-Uralic is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Uralic language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from shared features across descendant languages such as Finnic, Samoyedic, and Ugric. Scholars categorize it as a prehistoric, widely distributed linguistic stage dating before historical records.

Proto-Ugric is the reconstructed ancestor of the Ugric languages within the Uralic language family. It is reconstructed and not directly attested; knowledge comes from comparative reconstruction of its descendant languages.

Urak Lawoi' is an Austronesian language spoken by the Urak Lawoi' people of southern Thailand and the Malay Peninsula. It is recognized in linguistic references as a distinct language with its own morphology and lexicon, reflecting the unique linguistic heritage of its speakers.

Veps is a living language in the Finnic branch of the Uralic language family, spoken by the Veps people. It is used in parts of Russia and has an established historical presence in the region.

Vaghri is a language name used in linguistic references to denote a distinct language. It appears in a category of Vaghri language entries, indicating recognized usage within linguistic documentation while specific details such as status, region, or period are not provided here.

Virgin Islands Creole is a creole language spoken in the Virgin Islands. It is classified among Caribbean creole languages and arose from language contact in the Atlantic plantation zone during the colonial era. Its exact origins and status are not fully documented.

Korlai Creole Portuguese is a Portuguese-based creole language associated with Korlai in Maharashtra, India. It developed from contact between Portuguese and local Indian languages, forming a distinct speech form recognized as a creole. The language is documented as part of the regional linguistic landscape and historical contact.

Vulgar Latin is the spoken form of Latin that circulated among everyday speakers in the Roman world and gave rise to the Romance language family. It is not the classical literary Latin and is considered the ancestral stage for languages such as Italian, Spanish, and French.

West Flemish is a West Germanic language spoken in the western part of Belgium, in the province of West Flanders and nearby areas. It is part of the Dutch language continuum with distinctive local varieties and historical development reflecting regional usage.

East Franconian is a Germanic language variety spoken in eastern Franconia, a region of Bavaria in Germany. It is categorized within the Franconian language group and has a long history in local use, reflecting inherited dialectal forms from broader medieval and early modern periods.

Makhuwa is represented here as a language category on Wiktionary, linked to the Makhuwa language. This entry serves to group terms and etymologies associated with Makhuwa within a broad understanding of language categories. It functions as a navigational hub for related linguistic data.

Volapük is a constructed international auxiliary language created in the late 19th century in Germany. It was designed as a global means of communication and became the first widely promoted attempt to create a universal language, though it later declined in prominence.

Voro is the name of a language listed in a linguistic category. Votic is a Finnic language of the Uralic family, historically spoken by the Votic people in Ingria, near the Gulf of Finland in present-day Russia. It forms part of the Finnic subgroup of Uralic languages, related to other languages in the region.

Võro is a Finnic language in the Uralic language family. It is spoken in southern Estonia and forms part of the regional language landscape associated with the Estonian language group. Walloon is a Romance language of the Gallo-Romance branch spoken in the Walloon Region of Belgium. It has a long attested history and distinct regional varieties reflecting its use in local communities.

Wolaytta is an Afro-Asiatic language of the Cushitic branch associated with the Wolaytta people. The language is categorized under Wolaytta language in linguistic reference resources. It has documented lexical items and grammar in reference works.

Massachusett language is the historical language of the Massachusett people. It is an Algonquian language traditionally spoken in what is now Massachusetts, with attestations in historical documents and evidence of its classification within the broader language family.

Waray-Waray is a Philippine language of the Visayan branch within the Austronesian family. It is spoken on the islands of Samar and Leyte in the central Philippines and forms part of the Visayan subgroup of Central Philippine languages.

Wayana is a language categorized under the Wayana language category in linguistic references. It appears in discussions of language classification within the Wayana language group, with no claim of specific contemporary usage in this description.

Warao is a language spoken by the Warao people in northeastern Venezuela, primarily along the Orinoco Delta. The language is the focus of linguistic documentation and classification efforts, with limited historical records available and ongoing studies to understand its structure and relationships.

Waigali is a Nuristani language of Afghanistan, within the Indo-Iranian branch of the Indo-European language family. It is spoken in the Nuristan region of eastern Afghanistan, where several related Nuristani languages are found, reflecting a distinct branch of the broader Indo-Iranian linguistic tradition.

Wakhi is a language in the Wakhi language category. It is an Iranian language of the Indo-Iranian family, spoken by communities in Central Asia and the Pamir region, reflecting its status as a living language through ongoing lexical documentation and regional use today and locally.

Cameroon Pidgin is an English-based pidgin language spoken in Cameroon. It functions as a contact language in multilingual settings and serves as a lingua franca for local communication in the region. Its status is that of a pidgin, not a fully standardized national language.

Middle Welsh is the medieval stage of the Welsh language, a Brittonic member of the Celtic language family. It was spoken in Wales during the Middle Ages and is known from literary and documentary texts of that period.

Wolio is an Austronesian language spoken on Buton Island in Southeast Sulawesi, Indonesia. It is categorized in linguistic references as a language of the Wolio people and part of the wider Austronesian language family. Its classification follows standard linguistic taxonomy.

Wallisian is a Polynesian language in the Oceanic branch of the Austronesian language family. It is spoken on the Wallis and Futuna islands in the central Pacific. As part of the wider Polynesian languages, Wallisian shares linguistic features with nearby tongues and contributes to the cultural heritage of its speakers.

Wali (Ghana) is a language of Ghana. The available material lists it under a Wali (Ghana) language category, indicating its recognition as a distinct language. Wolof is a Niger-Congo language of the Senegambian branch, spoken in Senegal, The Gambia, and Mauritania. It is a living language with a long history in West Africa. As part of the Atlantic-Congo family, it forms one of the major languages of West Africa with a well-attested vocabulary and dialect diversity.

Wutunhua is a language listed in linguistic references under the Wutunhua language category. It is treated as a human language in scholarly overviews, with information primarily organized by categories rather than narrative descriptions, and the available material is concise and category-focused.

Wu is a Chinese language of the Sino-Tibetan family, spoken in eastern China, including Shanghai and parts of Zhejiang and Jiangsu. It comprises regional varieties with distinct phonology and vocabulary, reflecting a long historical presence in the region.

Vilamovian is a Germanic language spoken in southern Poland, notably around Wilamowice; it belongs to the Germanic language family and survives with a small speaker base as a heritage language in the region, with distinctive features tied to its locality.

Andalusian Arabic is a historical variety of Arabic in the Afro-Asiatic family, once spoken in Al-Andalus in the Iberian Peninsula during the medieval Islamic period. It reflects regional linguistic development within Arabic and Iberian contact.

Aequian is an extinct Italic language once spoken by the Aequi in ancient central Italy. It is part of the Italic branch of the Indo-European language family and is known from limited inscriptions and place-names, reflecting its sparse attestation in the historical record.

Kalmyk is a language of the Kalmyk people in the Kalmykia region of Russia. It is a Mongolic language belonging to the Mongolic language family and is documented as part of the linguistic inventory of the region.

Kamassian is an extinct Samoyedic language of the Uralic family, once spoken in Siberia. It is part of the Kamassian language category and is documented in historical linguistic records. The status is historical with limited socio-linguistic detail available.

Bactrian is an Eastern Iranian language in the Indo-Iranian language family. It was historically spoken in Bactria, a Central Asian region that roughly overlaps present-day Afghanistan and surrounding areas. The language is attested in ancient inscriptions and texts from Central Asia.

Middle Breton is the medieval stage of the Breton language, a Celtic language of the Brittonic branch spoken in Brittany, France. It represents an historical form used during the medieval period and is not a modern, living variety.

Bulgar is a language in the Slavic branch of the Indo-European language family, associated with Bulgaria in Southeast Europe. It is categorized within the Bulgarian language group and has historical roots tied to the South Slavic linguistic area.

Volga Bulgar is an extinct Turkic language of the Oghur branch formerly spoken along the Volga region in Russia. It is known from medieval records and from its legacy in loanwords and historical references of neighboring languages.

Cumbric is a historical Brittonic language of the Celtic family, associated with the Cumbria region in Britain during the early medieval period. It is primarily known from a small corpus of inscriptions and toponymic forms, with no long surviving literary tradition.

Celtiberian is an extinct Celtic language of the Iberian Peninsula, known from inscriptions dating to the 2nd century BCE to the 1st century CE. It belongs to the Celtic language family and is closely related to other ancient Celtiberian dialects.

Old Armenian is a historical stage of the Armenian language, part of the Indo-European language family and the Armenian branch. It represents an early form of Armenian used in antiquity within the Armenian cultural region and is not a living language today.

Khwarezmian is an extinct Eastern Iranian language formerly spoken in the Khwarezm region of Central Asia; it is part of the Iranian language family and is known from medieval sources. Its records are limited to inscriptions and textual fragments from medieval times. It has no living speakers.

Carian is an extinct Anatolian language within the Indo-European language family. It was spoken in the ancient region of Caria in southwestern Anatolia and is known from inscriptions and occasional glosses that survive from the classical and late antique periods. It is related to other Anatolian languages such as Lydian and Phrygian.

Classical Tibetan is the literary and liturgical form of the Tibetan language, used in Tibet and surrounding regions from about the 7th to the 11th centuries. It belongs to the Tibetic branch of the Sino-Tibetan language family.

Dacian is an extinct Indo-European language of the ancient Dacians, known mainly from limited inscriptions and onomastic evidence. Its classification within Indo-European is debated, and it is not directly attested by a substantial corpus, leaving the exact subgroup criteria unresolved.

Dharug is the language of the Dharug people in the Sydney region of New South Wales, Australia. It belongs to the Pama-Nyungan language family and is documented in linguistic sources with a focus on historical forms and lexical materials.

Faliscan is an extinct Italic language of the Latino-Faliscan subgroup within the Indo-European family. It was spoken in ancient central Italy, around Falerii, from the early Iron Age into the Roman era, and is closely related to Latin.

Galatian is an extinct Indo-European language of the Celtic branch, historically spoken by the Galatians in central Anatolia during antiquity. It belongs to the Continental Celtic subgroup related to Gaulish, and is known from limited ancient evidence rather than ongoing use.

Proto-Mongolic is the reconstructed ancestor of the Mongolic language family. It is not directly attested and is inferred from comparative analysis of descendant languages within the Mongolic group. As a proto-language, its status is reconstructed rather than documented by direct written records.

Xhosa is a language listed in the Xhosa language category. Hunnic is an extinct language associated with the Huns. It is poorly attested, with evidence limited to a small corpus in late antiquity. Middle Khmer is a historical form of the Khmer language. It represents a stage in the development of Khmer and is categorized as a historical variety used in the past. It is studied in etymology to trace sound changes and vocabulary evolution.

Hurrian is an ancient language of the Near East, known from historical records and linguistic study. It is listed under the Hurrian language category, indicating its identity as a distinct language rather than a dialect or extinct branch.

Iberian is a language associated with the Iberian Peninsula. It is listed in linguistic reference materials under the Iberian language category, reflecting its recognition as a distinct language historically; details such as its status, family, and period are not specified here.

Illyrian is a historical language name used in linguistic documentation. Lycian is an extinct Indo-European language of the Luwic branch of the Anatolian family, formerly spoken in Lycia, southwestern Anatolia. It is known from inscriptions dating from the 5th century BCE to the 4th century CE and is not directly attested as a living language.

Lydian is an ancient Indo-European language of the Anatolian branch, historically spoken by the Lydians in western Anatolia (the region of Lydia). It is extinct and attested from inscriptions dating to the first millennium BCE, providing part of the linguistic record of ancient Anatolia.

Alanic is an extinct Iranian language of the Iranic branch. Loup A is a language identified by the name Loup A. It is categorized under the Loup A language category, indicating it is recognized as a distinct linguistic variety within a naming scheme used to group related terms. No regional or historical specifics are stated.

Lepontic is an ancient Italic language spoken by the Lepontii in the Alpine region of northern Italy and southern Switzerland. Lusitanian is an extinct ancient language once spoken in the Iberian Peninsula. It is known from inscriptions and related linguistic material, and its precise affiliations within the Indo-European family remain debated.
Luwian is a language name used in linguistic references. It is listed in a category for the Luwian language, serving as a label for comparative and historical discussions.

Elymian is the language attributed to the Elymians, identified in historical records and scholarly references. Kermanic is a language category identified as part of the Kermanic language grouping. Old Median language is a historical language category. It is associated with the ancient Median linguistic tradition in the Iranian language area. The name indicates an early stage in the Median group; the exact period and geographic scope are not precisely fixed in the available sources.

Mingrelian is a Kartvelian language of the South Caucasian family spoken in western Georgia, chiefly in the Mingrelia region. It is related to Georgian and Svan within the Kartvelian branch and is attested as a living language in modern Georgia.

Ancient Macedonian is an extinct language of the ancient Macedon region in northern Greece. It is linked to the Hellenic branch of the Indo-European family and is known from inscriptions and historical references dating to classical antiquity.

Manado Malay is a Malay language variety listed in linguistic references. It belongs to the Malayic branch of the Austronesian language family and is associated with the Manado region. Manichaean Middle Persian is the Middle Persian variety used by the Manichaean movement. It is part of the Iranian language family and is known from historic religious and scholarly contexts associated with Manichaeism in former territories.

Proto-Na-Dene is the reconstructed common ancestor of the Na-Dene language family in North America; it is not directly attested and is inferred from its descendant languages. The Na-Dene family includes Athabaskan and related languages today.

Middle Mongol is a historical stage of the Mongolic language family spoken in parts of Central Asia during the medieval period. It is referred to as a distinct stage in linguistic classification and is not a currently spoken vernacular.

Anglo-Norman is a Romance language variety that originated from Norman French and was used in medieval England. It belongs to the Indo-European language family and contributed to English vocabulary and place names through Norman rule and cultural contact.

Narragansett is an Algonquian language historically spoken by the Narragansett people in the area of present-day Rhode Island. Phrygian is an extinct Indo-European language spoken in ancient Phrygia in western Anatolia. It forms part of the Phrygian branch of Indo-European and is known from inscriptions dating from the first millennium BCE to the early centuries CE.

Pumpokol is a language name used in linguistic references. The available information does not provide details on its language family, region, or historical period.

Mohegan-Pequot is an Algonquian language of the Mohegan-Pequot subgroup. It was spoken by the Mohegan and Pequot peoples in the region of present-day Connecticut in New England and is part of the broader Algonquian language family.

Parthian is an extinct Northwestern Iranian language of the Iranian language family, once spoken in Parthia, an ancient region in the northeast of Iran and adjacent areas in Mesopotamia. It is known from inscriptions and late antique texts dating to antiquity.

Punic is a Semitic language of the Phoenician branch, historically spoken in the Carthaginian sphere in North Africa. It is part of the Afro-Asiatic language family and is known from ancient inscriptions and records, reflecting its use in classical antiquity and its status as a historical, not contemporary, language.

Karakhanid is a Turkic language of the Karluk branch. It was historically spoken in Central Asia, particularly in areas linked to the Karakhanid state and its cultural sphere. Sambali is a language identified by a category labeled Sambali language. The canonical name is Sambali, indicating a distinct linguistic identity included in language classification resources. Proto-Sarmatian is a reconstructed, not directly attested proto-language and the reconstructed common ancestor of the Sarmatian languages within the Iranian language family. Proto-Saka-Wakhi is a reconstructed proto-language, not directly attested, identified as the common ancestor of the Saka and Wakhi language varieties. It belongs to the Iranian branch of the Indo-Iranian family and is inferred through comparative evidence among its descendants.

Alcozauca Mixtec is a Mixtecan language in the Oto-Manguean language family. Transalpine Gaulish is a Gaulish language of the Continental Celtic family, once spoken in the Transalpine region of Gaul. It is extinct and known from ancient inscriptions and place names, with its features helping establish connections within the Celtic language family.

Magdalena Peñasco Mixtec is a Mixtec language of the Oto-Manguean language family. It is documented as a Mixtec variety associated with the Magdalena Peñasco area, reflecting its place within the Mixtec branch of the broader Oto-Manguean languages in the region.

Tocharian A is a language categorized as Tocharian_A_language. It is part of the Tocharian language group and is documented in linguistic category listings that group Tocharian A with related languages of the Tocharian branch.

Umbrian is an ancient Italic language of the Indo-European family, historically spoken in central Italy around the Umbrian region. It is known from inscriptions and literary fragments dating to the early Iron Age through classical antiquity.

Venetic is an extinct Italic language of the Indo-European family spoken by the Veneti in northeast Italy. It is attested in inscriptions dated roughly from the 6th to the 1st century BCE, written in the Venetic alphabet and representing a localized Italic linguistic variety.

Vandalic is an extinct East Germanic language in the Germanic language family. It was historically associated with the Vandals in late antiquity and is known primarily from limited historical references and inscription material, with no living native speakers.

Volscian is an extinct Italic language of the Volsci in ancient Latium, central Italy. It belongs to the Italic branch of the Indo-European language family. Written Oirat is a language in the Mongolic family, specifically the Oirat branch. It has a written tradition and is used to represent Oirat speech in textual form. Yámana is a human language associated with the Yámana people, listed in a Wiktionary category for Yámana language. The entry does not provide extensive details on its language family or regional spread.

Yazghulami is an Iranian language of the Pamir sub-branch, historically spoken by the Yazghul people in the remote highlands of Central Asia. The language is documented under the Yazghulami language, with limited contemporary usage and uncertain vitality in modern communities.

Yaghnobi is an Eastern Iranian language spoken by the Yaghnobi people in Tajikistan. Yaqui is a human language spoken by the Yaqui people in western Sonora, Mexico, and in southern Arizona. It is a member of the Uto-Aztecan language family and is associated with Yaqui communities in the region, transmitted within families and communities across generations.

Yidgha is a language with its own entry in the Yidgha_language category, representing a distinct linguistic system. It is documented as a human language with unique lexical items and structure, reflecting its status as a separate language rather than a mere variant of another tongue.

Yevanic is a Judaeo-Greek language in the Greek language family, historically used by Jewish communities in Greece. It combines Greek lexicon with Hebrew loanwords and distinctive religious terms, reflecting a Greek-speaking Jewish culture in the Hellenic world.

Yiddish is a language categorized under Yiddish language. It is treated as a human language with availability of etymological data and word histories for research into language development and historical contact.

Yakan is an Austronesian language of the Philippines. Mysian is a language identified as Mysian language in linguistic categorization. The available information in the category emphasizes its classification rather than descriptive features, with no verifiable data on status, regional range, or historical period provided within this entry.

Yoruba is a living Niger-Congo language of the Yoruboid subgroup spoken by the Yoruba people in southwestern Nigeria and adjacent areas of Benin and Togo. It has a long documented history and a wide repertoire of dialects and regional varieties.

Yogad is a language categorized under the Yogad language category. Yola is an extinct form of English historically spoken in Ireland, primarily in the region of County Wexford. It is part of the West Germanic branch of the Indo-European language family, related to English; the language is not attested in modern use and dates to medieval and early modern periods.

Nheengatu is the name used for a language documented in linguistic resources. This entry points to a collection of related terms and lexical data categorized under Nheengatu language, illustrating usage across a range of lexemes.

Yucatec Maya is a Mayan language in the Maya language family, spoken in the Yucatán Peninsula of Mexico. It is documented as a distinct language within the Yucatec Maya language category, reflecting its long-standing linguistic heritage in Mesoamerica.

Cantonese is a language variety listed under the Cantonese language category. The entry collection includes terms and references associated with Cantonese, reflecting its recognition as a distinct linguistic variety within the broader language group.

Yug is a language that appears in the Yug language category. The available source material provides no explicit details about its status, genetic classification, regional distribution, or historical period, so any such attributes should be treated as unverified pending further documentation.

Yurok is a language of the Yurok people in northwestern California.
